findings.claim: This change FIXES a hang rather than introducing one. Previously force_initialize created a tracker 'values' tensor of size num_layers, while the router's save_to_aux_losses_tracker (and downstream num_moe_layers computation) uses num_layers + mtp_num_layers when MTP is enabled. PP ranks with no MoE layers would create a tensor of size num_layers and then perform an all_reduce across the PP group against ranks whose tensors are size num_layers+mtp_num_layers, causing a mismatched-size collective and a hang. The fix makes tracker_num_layers = num_layers + mtp_num_layers consistent across ranks. No new performance cost is introduced (a marginally larger zeros tensor of a few elements on the empty-MoE ranks).

The default of --rl-persist-cuda-graphs is flipped from True to False in arguments.py. Per the help text and the CUDA-graph comment block in validate_args, when this flag is False 'CUDA graphs are deleted on suspend and re-captured on resume.' In the RL loop, inference and training phases alternate (suspend/resume) once per RL iteration, so with the new default the engine will delete and re-capture ALL inference CUDA graphs on every iteration. CUDA graph capture requires running dummy forward passes for each captured batch-size bucket (inference_dynamic_batching_num_cuda_graphs can be many buckets, and PR #3527 recently added finer-grained graphs), making recapture a heavy recurring cost. Any RL job that previously relied on the default (True) and does not pass the flag explicitly will now pay this per-iteration recapture overhead, degrading end-to-end throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an RL rollout/training loop with cuda-graph-impl=local and measure per-iteration wall time and GPU-idle time during resume, comparing --rl-persist-cuda-graphs vs --no-rl-persist-cuda-graphs; count CUDA graph capture events per iteration.

findings.claim: The change also propagates to InferenceConfig.static_kv_memory_pointers (megatron/inference/utils.py:302 sets static_kv_memory_pointers=args.rl_persist_cuda_graphs). With the new default False, static_kv_memory_pointers becomes False by default, which may change KV-cache allocation strategy and prevent the memory-pointer stability optimization used for graph reuse. The common.sh update restores True for the RL example, but other callers relying on the old default lose this optimization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare KV-cache allocation path and inference decode latency with static_kv_memory_pointers True vs False.

findings.claim: In save_kv_to_host (d2h) and the h2d receive path, the single fused copy_blocks call was replaced by a Python for-loop that issues one copy_blocks call per KV cache group. For hybrid (HMA) models with multiple groups, this multiplies the number of kernel launches / copy invocations per request by the number of groups, and prevents coalescing of the I/O across groups. The code itself flags this: 'TODO (NickLucche) D2H<>H2D ops could benefit from coalescing io across groups'. This increases per-transfer latency on the host<->device staging path proportional to num_groups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: P/D transfer with cpu kv_buffer_device (use_host_buffer=True) on an HMA/hybrid model (e.g. sliding-window + full attention groups); measure per-request d2h/h2d copy latency and end-to-end transfer time vs a single-group model.

findings.claim: Two logger.debug calls for attention backend and kv cache layout detection were promoted to logger.info. These are one-time init-path logs (register_kv_caches), so the runtime impact is negligible, but it does emit unconditional log output at INFO in a path that was previously gated by debug level.

get_sw_clipped_blocks builds a new tuple/list per request via a list comprehension on the scheduler side and is now invoked on the recv, save, and request_finished paths. For non-hybrid models it early-returns (guarded by self._is_hma_required), so the cost is bounded, but for hybrid models it adds per-request slicing/allocation over all KV cache groups on the scheduler critical path.

By gating skinny-gemm dispatch on `x.is_contiguous()` and removing the prior `rocm_enforce_contiguous_skinny_gemm_inputs` (which cloned to contiguous), any non-contiguous activation now falls back to `torch.nn.functional.linear` instead of the specialized wvSplitK/LLMM1/wvSplitKrc skinny kernels. For small-n (decode) skinny GEMM shapes, the specialized kernels are typically faster than the generic hipBLASLt path, so callers that pass a non-contiguous `x` (e.g. sliced/strided activations) will lose the skinny-kernel speedup. Net effect is workload-dependent: it saves a clone (memory + copy) but may run a slower matmul for those inputs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On MI300/gfx9, benchmark rocm_unquantized_gemm and the fp8 scaled_mm path with a non-contiguous (strided) activation tensor of a typical decode skinny shape (n=1..4, large k) and compare latency before vs after this change (clone+skinny kernel vs generic linear).

findings.claim: Removing `rocm_enforce_contiguous_skinny_gemm_inputs` eliminates the forced `.contiguous()` clones on every LLMM1/wvSplitK/wvSplitKrc/wvSplitKQ call. For the common case of already-contiguous inputs this was a no-op, so the change removes the (small) per-call is_contiguous check overhead and avoids clones on non-contiguous inputs, i.e. a slight net improvement rather than a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per-call Python overhead of the skinny gemm wrappers before/after (contiguous inputs) to confirm negligible change.

findings.claim: When num_cuda_graphs == -1, the new auto-mode enumerates a very large, fixed set of token counts: [1,2,4] + range(8,256,8) (31 entries) + range(256, cuda_graph_max_tokens+1, 16). For a typical cuda_graph_max_tokens (e.g. 8192 or 16384), this produces on the order of 500-1000+ distinct token-count buckets. Each token count is turned into decode/mixed/prefill batch dimensions and each is captured as a separate CUDA graph. Capturing hundreds of CUDA graphs dramatically increases startup/warmup time (each capture is a full model forward pass) and, more importantly, consumes large amounts of GPU memory for the persistent graph pools and static input/output buffers, potentially reducing memory available for KV cache. The old behavior clamped num_cuda_graphs to a user-chosen small number; '-1' silently produces far more graphs than any previous config.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CUDA graph capture (warmup) wall-clock time and peak GPU memory for num_cuda_graphs=-1 vs num_cuda_graphs=4 at cuda_graph_max_tokens=8192, counting the number of graphs actually captured.

findings.claim: The much larger cuda_graph_batch_dimensions_list produced in -1 mode increases the cost of per-step graph matching (match_graph_config iterates / searches over the candidate list) and the set/sort operations in generate_cuda_graph_batch_dimensions_list. While per-step matching is minor relative to model compute, a list that is an order of magnitude larger adds constant per-decode-step CPU overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark match_graph_config latency with the batch-dimension list sizes generated by num_cuda_graphs=4 vs -1.

findings.claim: The coarse step of 16 tokens beyond 256 (range(256, max+1, 16)) means small-batch coverage is fine-grained but large batches still round up to the nearest 16 tokens; this is intentional for coverage but the extremely fine granularity below 256 (steps of 1,2,4,8) may over-capture graphs that are rarely hit while providing negligible padding savings, wasting capture time/memory for little runtime benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the distribution of matched graph token counts on a representative inference workload to see how often the fine-grained small buckets are actually selected.

findings.claim: The default value of VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TRITON_ROPE is flipped from True to False. On ROCm gfx9 (MI300X etc.) this disables the AITER Triton fused rotary-embedding kernel by default. As a result RotaryEmbeddingBase.use_aiter (base.py: `self.use_aiter = self.enabled() and rocm_aiter_ops.is_triton_rotary_embed_enabled()`) becomes False, so forward_hip falls back to forward_cuda / the generic torch custom op path for RoPE instead of the tuned AITER Triton kernel. RoPE is applied on the query/key of every attention layer for every token in every forward pass, so replacing the fused kernel with the fallback path adds measurable per-layer per-step latency for all ROCm decode/prefill workloads unless the user explicitly re-enables the env var. This is a deliberate bugfix (kernel presumably numerically/functionally broken), but the runtime effect is a throughput/latency regression on the previously-fast default path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a gfx942/MI300X ROCm build, measure decode tokens/s and per-step latency for a 7B/70B model with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TRITON_ROPE=1 (old default) vs =0 (new default), isolating the RoPE kernel time via a profiler.

findings.claim: In rocm.py the condition guarding registration of the '+rotary_embedding' custom op was changed from `use_aiter_triton_rope` (i.e. is_triton_rotary_embed_enabled()) to the broader `rocm_aiter_ops.is_enabled()`. This means the rotary_embedding customop is now force-enabled whenever AITER is generally enabled, even though the AITER Triton RoPE kernel is now off by default. RotaryEmbeddingBase.forward_hip will then take the customop dispatch path but with use_aiter=False, meaning it routes to forward_cuda (the standard ops.rotary_embedding). This does not itself add a fast kernel; it may keep the op out of the torch.compile-fused graph (forcing a separate custom-op call instead of a fusion), potentially preventing RoPE+KV-cache fusion passes that key off is_triton_rotary_embed_enabled(). This could reduce fusion opportunities and add a kernel-launch boundary per layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare torch.compile fused-graph RoPE handling (number of separate kernel launches per layer / presence of rope-kvcache fusion) with is_enabled()-based registration vs the old is_triton_rotary_embed_enabled()-based registration on ROCm.

findings.claim: The fast path that dispatched BF16 1D-scaling NVFP4 quantize+transpose to the tuned kernel (quantize_transpose_tuned_1D) is commented out. Every such call now falls through to the generic quantize_transpose_nvfp4_kernel. This path is exercised for the common NVFP4 training case in both quantize_fwd_helper and quantize_bwd_helper (NVTE_NVFP4_1D_SCALING with dtype==kBFloat16, rows%32==0, cols%32==0, nvfp4_2d_quantization==false). The tuned kernel was written specifically for higher throughput (tuned 64x64 tiles, prefetch/persistent-kernel scaffolding, and optional fast-math bf16 scaling coefficients), so disabling it degrades NVFP4 cast-transpose throughput for the dominant BF16 workload. The condition guarding the tuned kernel (input.dtype()==kBFloat16 && !use_2d_quantization) shows exactly which workloads regress.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark nvfp4 quantize_transpose (NVTE_NVFP4_1D_SCALING, BF16 input, rows/cols multiples of 32, 2D quantization off, return_transpose=true) on sm100+, comparing kernel time with the tuned dispatch enabled vs. the commented-out fallback to quantize_transpose_nvfp4_kernel.

findings.claim: The tuned kernel supported a USE_FAST_MATH scaling-coefficient path (bf16 scaling coefficient via SCALING_COEFFICIENT_TYPE<true> and mul_cvt_bf16_to_fp4_8x_* fast-math intrinsics). Disabling the tuned kernel removes access to that faster scaling-coefficient computation for NVFP4 quantization; the generic kernel computes block_scale_inverse in fp32 and does not use the fast-math bf16 coefficient path, adding per-block arithmetic cost. The corresponding test change also drops use_fast_nvfp4_scaling_vec={false,true} down to only {false}, confirming the fast-math variant is no longer used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-block scaling-coefficient compute cost / kernel time for NVFP4 quantize with fast-math bf16 coefficients (tuned kernel) vs. fp32 coefficients (generic kernel) on representative training tensor shapes.

findings.claim: Adding `and cfg.parallel_config.pipeline_parallel_size == 1` disables the allreduce+RMS-norm fusion pass whenever pipeline parallelism (PP>1) is used. In PP>1 deployments that otherwise met the criteria (TP>1, Hopper/Blackwell, flashinfer), the fused allreduce+RMSNorm kernel will no longer be emitted, so those layers fall back to a separate all-reduce followed by a separate RMSNorm, forgoing the kernel-fusion / communication-overlap benefit. This is a minor per-layer throughput/latency cost for PP>1 setups. However, it is an intentional correctness bugfix (issue #35426 reports the tp-pp fusion is broken), so leaving the fusion enabled is not a valid alternative — the change is the correct trade-off and only affects the PP>1 path, leaving the common PP=1 path unchanged.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokens/sec and per-step latency for a TP>1, PP>1 configuration on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer, before vs after this change, to quantify the fusion-loss overhead (and verify correctness is restored).

findings.claim: TensorIpcReceiver.__call__ calls self.queue.get(timeout=10.0) synchronously inside the engine-core input IO thread (via MsgpackDecoder's oob_tensor_provider). If the expected tensor has not yet arrived on the multiprocessing.Queue (e.g. sender slow, tensor dropped, or IDs mismatch), this blocks the entire input thread for up to 10s per missing tensor, stalling all request intake and effectively hanging request processing. Because it loops draining until the specific (sender_id, message_id, tensor_id) is found, a single lost/misordered tensor makes each iteration re-block for another 10s.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under torch_shm mode, measure request add latency and engine input-thread throughput when multimodal tensors arrive with induced delay/reordering; compare to direct_rpc.

findings.claim: TensorIpcSender.__call__ runs inside MsgpackEncoder.enc_hook (the serialization hot path used by the client to submit requests) and does a blocking self.queue.put(ipc_data, timeout=10.0). If the engine-side consumer is slow to drain the multiprocessing.Queue, the queue's internal buffer/pipe fills and put() blocks the client's request-encoding path for up to 10s, adding tail latency and reducing request submission throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark request submission latency in torch_shm mode with a backlog on the engine side (consumer paused) versus normal, measuring encode-path stall time.

findings.claim: For every multimodal tensor sent, the sender calls tensor.share_memory_() when not already shared. For CPU tensors this relocates the tensor into a shared-memory segment (an extra full copy of the tensor data) on the request submission path. This is per-tensor overhead that grows with multimodal payload size and request rate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request CPU time and memory copies in torch_shm mode for large image/video multimodal tensors vs direct_rpc.

findings.claim: _encode_tensor now gates the inline small-tensor fast path on 'obj.is_cpu' (previously just nbytes < threshold). Any small non-CPU tensor now falls through to the aux-buffer branch which calls tensor_data(obj) (flatten().contiguous().view(uint8).numpy()); for non-CPU tensors .numpy() cannot succeed and for borderline cases this changes the code path for the always-on default (direct_rpc) encoder, potentially adding a contiguous/copy step for tensors that used to be inlined.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MsgpackEncoder on small CPU vs non-CPU tensors before/after this change.

findings.claim: logger.warning("Discarding %d stale tensors from sender %s", sender_id) passes only one argument for two format specifiers, which raises a logging formatting error. This lives in the stale-tensor discard branch of the receiver's drain loop; the resulting exception/logging overhead occurs exactly when the queue is under contention/reordering, worsening the stall path.

The OOV text-embedding path in `_embed_text_input_ids` was changed from embedding only the *text-token subset* (`embed_input_ids(input_ids[is_text])` on the compacted subset) to embedding the *entire* sequence (`embed_input_ids(input_ids.masked_fill(is_multimodal, 0))`). For OOV models with a large fraction of multimodal placeholder tokens (image/video/audio placeholders often dominate the sequence, e.g. hundreds-to-thousands of vision tokens per image), this makes the embedding gather/lookup operate on N total tokens instead of only the (often much smaller) text-token count. The embedding lookup and any per-token work (e.g. LoRA embedding, all-reduce for VocabParallelEmbedding) now scales with the full padded sequence length rather than the text subset, increasing embedding-layer compute and, for tensor-parallel vocab-parallel embeddings, communication volume.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark embed_input_ids on a Gemma3/LLaVA-Next/Ultravox-style batch where >90% of tokens are multimodal placeholders (e.g. 1 image = ~1000 placeholder tokens + 20 text tokens), comparing parent (subset embedding + masked_scatter) vs new (full masked_fill + full embedding) latency and TP all-reduce bytes.

findings.claim: The new code allocates a full-length copy of `input_ids` via `input_ids.masked_fill(is_multimodal, 0)` on every forward for OOV models. The old code allocated an empty output buffer and a compacted subset tensor. The relative memory footprint depends on token dtype vs embedding dtype; the masked_fill copy is int64 of size N, which is generally smaller than the old empty float buffer of size N x hidden, so this is likely a net memory improvement, but it still introduces an unconditional per-step allocation/copy of the input_ids tensor on the OOV path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak activation memory and allocator churn for the embed step on an OOV multimodal model between parent and this commit.

findings.claim: In `qwen2_5_omni_thinker.py::embed_input_ids`, the code now unconditionally computes full text embeddings via `_embed_text_input_ids(...)` before checking `len(multimodal_embeddings) == 0`, and in the interleaved/non-interleaved branches it re-invokes `_embed_text_input_ids` again. When the interleaved path is taken this can result in the text embedding being computed twice (once for the early `inputs_embeds` and again inside the branch), duplicating the embedding lookup for that step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Qwen2.5-Omni thinker embed_input_ids on a use_audio_in_video=True batch and count/time calls to embed_input_ids to confirm whether text embedding is computed twice.

findings.claim: `configure_mm_token_handling` unconditionally emits `logger.info(...)` at model init. This is per-init (not per-step) so runtime impact is negligible, but at info level it adds noise; not a runtime hot-path concern.

The change swaps the UCX memory-leak mitigation from disabling mmap hooks (UCX_MEM_MMAP_HOOK_MODE=none) to capping unreleased registration-cache entries (UCX_RCACHE_MAX_UNRELEASED=1024). Bounding the rcache's unreleased entries to 1024 can force more frequent memory deregistration/re-registration for NIXL KV transfers on workloads that churn through many distinct buffers, which may reduce transfer throughput compared to leaving the rcache unbounded. However, the previous setting also had performance implications, and the exact effect depends on UCX internals and workload; this is a plausible but unquantified tradeoff, not a definite regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NixlConnector PD KV-transfer throughput/latency benchmark comparing UCX_MEM_MMAP_HOOK_MODE=none vs UCX_RCACHE_MAX_UNRELEASED=1024 under high request rate with many distinct KV blocks.

findings.claim: Pre/postprocessing is now offloaded to `models.renderer._executor` (make_async(self._preprocessing, executor=self._executor) and self._postprocessing). This is the *same* ThreadPoolExecutor that the renderer uses for blocking tokenizer encode/decode (AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer) and multimodal preprocessing. `_preprocessing` calls `pre_process_online` -> `renderer.render_cmpl`/`render_chat`, which themselves perform (synchronous) tokenization on that pool's threads. If the pool has few workers (renderer_num_workers, potentially 1 for the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer path) a preprocessing task can occupy a worker while its own downstream tokenization work is queued on the same pool, reducing throughput and increasing latency under concurrency, and risking pool starvation. Sharing one bounded pool for both the outer preprocessing task and the inner tokenization it triggers can serialize requests that were previously interleaved on the event loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent embedding/pooling request benchmark (e.g. 100+ concurrent /v1/embeddings requests) measuring p50/p99 latency and throughput, comparing shared-executor vs a dedicated preprocessing pool, and varying renderer_num_workers.

findings.claim: Previously pre_process_online_async/post_process_online_async were plain async wrappers running inline on the event loop; render_cmpl internally already offloaded blocking tokenization via the async tokenizer's batching path. By wrapping the whole `pre_process_online` in make_async on a thread and having it call the *synchronous* render path, the request loses the micro-batching benefit of AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer (which batches encode calls across concurrent requests). If the sync render path uses blocking tokenizer.encode per request instead of the batched async tokenizer, aggregate tokenization throughput under concurrency may drop versus the batched approach.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokenization throughput of many concurrent short-prompt embedding requests before/after, checking whether encode calls are batched.

findings.claim: pooling_params is now always computed eagerly in _init_ctx via io_processor.create_pooling_params(request) for every request, whereas before it was only set if not already provided (the `if ctx.pooling_params is None` guard). This is minor per-request overhead but is now unconditional.

When prefix caching is enabled, get_active_used() and get_paused_used() now compute torch.unique(valid_ids) over the request_to_kv_block_ids matrix slice on the GPU. torch.unique performs a sort + dedup and forces a device sync when .numel() is read back to Python. Previously these were simple .sum().item() over the compact request_kv_block_counts vector. get_active_used feeds get_active_avail(), which is called on the request-scheduling path (dynamic_context.py:2026), and both are called every step from dynamic_engine.py metrics collection (lines 1443-1444). This adds a per-step GPU sort over an O(max_requests * max_kv_block_count) tensor plus a device->host sync, degrading decode-step scheduling latency when prefix caching is on.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step latency with enable_prefix_caching=True and a large max_requests * max_kv_block_count, measuring per-step time in get_active_avail / metrics collection vs. the old sum().item() path.

findings.claim: With num_speculative_tokens > 0, two additional large device tensors are allocated per hybrid model: mamba_intermediate_conv_states and mamba_intermediate_ssm_states each of shape (num_mamba_layers, max_requests, num_speculative_tokens+1, *state_shape). This multiplies the mamba intermediate state memory by (num_speculative_tokens+1) over the base per-layer state. For OFFLOAD mode, matching pinned CPU backups are also allocated (torch.empty_like(...).pin_memory()), doubling the host pinned-memory footprint as well. The memory accounting in mamba_states_memory_per_request was updated but the absolute increase can be substantial for large max_requests / speculative depth, reducing the KV-cache block budget.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU and pinned-host memory footprint of DynamicInferenceContext for a hybrid model at num_speculative_tokens=0 vs >0, and resulting KV block_count.

findings.claim: cuda_graph_max_tokens is now scaled by (num_speculative_tokens+1), and both decode-only and mixed batch-dimension generation loops now emit token counts scaled by (num_speculative_tokens+1). This can increase the number of distinct valid batch dimensions and therefore the number of CUDA graphs captured, increasing capture time and graph-pool memory. The dedup via set() mitigates but the token-count granularity is finer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count captured CUDA graphs and total capture time for num_speculative_tokens=0 vs >0 at fixed max_requests/num_cuda_graphs.

findings.claim: In the dummy-request initialization for CUDA graph warmup, token_to_local_position_within_kv_block and token_to_request_idx are now built with torch.arange(T) % tokens_per_request and torch.repeat_interleave, replacing the previous constant fills over N. This runs only during warmup/graph construction (not per decode step), so impact is limited, but it introduces extra device allocations during capture.

smallest_cuda_graph_dimensions now uses min() over a Python list comprehension filtering for prefill_req_count == 0 instead of min() over the full list. This is initialization-time only and negligible, but it materializes a new filtered list each call.

`import pandas as pd` was added at module top-level of `vllm/_aiter_ops.py`. This module is imported eagerly (non-lazily) by many core modules that are loaded on every vLLM startup regardless of platform (e.g. vllm/model_executor/layers/layernorm.py, fused_moe/layer.py, v1/attention/backends/triton_attn.py, mla_attention.py, topk_topp_sampler.py, etc.). Every other place in the vLLM codebase imports pandas lazily (inside functions or under TYPE_CHECKING) precisely because pandas is a heavy dependency (hundreds of ms of import time and large memory footprint). Making it a hard top-level import forces the pandas import cost onto every process that imports _aiter_ops, including CUDA/CPU deployments that never touch aiter, increasing engine/worker startup latency and resident memory. pandas is only needed inside `_load_gemm_tuned_configs`, so it should be imported lazily there.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `python -c "import vllm._aiter_ops"` cold import time and RSS before/after moving the pandas import inside _load_gemm_tuned_configs; also measure total engine cold-start time.

findings.claim: `_check_kernel_tuned` reconstructs the `l_m` candidate list (~90 elements, via range() and list comprehension) and performs up to ~90 set-membership lookups on every call. It is invoked from `can_implement` for each ROCm fp8 linear layer during kernel selection at model load. While `_load_gemm_tuned_configs` is cached, the list rebuild and membership scan are not; for models with many linear layers this adds a small, repeated O(90) cost at load time. Impact is confined to weight-loading/initialization (not the inference hot path), so it is minor, but the `l_m` list could be hoisted to a module-level constant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time model load for a large fp8 model on ROCm with many linear layers before/after hoisting l_m to a constant.

findings.claim: The default for VLLM_FLASHINFER_ALLREDUCE_BACKEND is changed from "auto" (which resolves to "mnnvl") to "trtllm". The adjacent comment in the same file states that "mnnvl performs mostly same/better than trtllm". Therefore, for the standalone all-reduce path on hardware where mnnvl is available and works, this change selects a backend that is at best equal and often marginally slower, i.e. a small potential all-reduce throughput/latency regression in the common non-quantized case. Note this is an intentional tradeoff: the change comment references issue #35772 where mnnvl is broken under CUDA graph capture, and a broken/disabled cudagraph path would be a far larger performance loss (or hang) than the marginal per-allreduce difference. So on cudagraph-enabled deployments this is net-positive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_device_communicators.py comparing VLLM_FLASHINFER_ALLREDUCE_BACKEND=mnnvl vs trtllm for standalone allreduce across world sizes and message sizes, both with and without cudagraph capture enabled.

findings.claim: When custom ops are disabled (the default under Inductor, custom_ops=['none']), dispatch_forward now returns `self.maybe_compile(self.forward_native, enable=compile_native)`. For SiluAndMul (activation.py) and QuantFP8 (input_quant_fp8.py) the default is compile_native=True, so their forward_native gets wrapped in an *independent* torch.compile(dynamic=True) region. But these ops are overwhelmingly instantiated as sub-modules of model MLP/attention layers (dozens of models: llama, qwen2/3, deepseek_v2, mixtral, etc. -- see the ~50 `self.act_fn = SiluAndMul()` sites) that are ALREADY inside the model-level torch.compile graph. Nesting an inner torch.compile inside the outer compiled region forces a graph break at each SiluAndMul/QuantFP8 call and prevents Inductor from fusing silu+mul (and the following quant) with surrounding elementwise ops -- exactly the fusion the activation_quant_fusion pass targets. This degrades throughput and GPU utilization for the common case, contrary to the intent stated in the docstring ('opaque custom ops should still be unwrapped wherever possible'), because the default is applied unconditionally rather than only when actually inside an opaque custom op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode/prefill throughput for a Llama-class model with default compilation (VLLM_COMPILE, custom_ops unset => 'none'); compare tokens/s and count of graph breaks before vs after. Also microbench SiluAndMul.forward under a torch.compiled parent module.

findings.claim: maybe_compile calls torch.compile(fn, dynamic=True, backend=simple_compile_backend) per CustomOp *instance*. SiluAndMul/QuantFP8 are instantiated once per layer, so a model with N transformer layers creates N separately-wrapped torch.compile functions (one per act_fn / quant instance). Even with dynamic=True to reduce recompiles, each distinct wrapped callable is a separate compilation unit that Inductor must trace/compile at first use, increasing model warmup/first-token latency and process memory (compiled artifacts) proportionally to layer count. Sharing a single compiled function (e.g. compiling the unbound forward_native once at class level) would avoid this per-instance multiplication.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model init + first-forward (warmup) wall time and peak host memory for a 32-64 layer model with custom_ops='none' and VLLM_COMPILE, before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: The wrapped forward_native is compiled with dynamic=True which mitigates shape-driven recompiles, but QuantFP8.forward_native branches on static vs dynamic and group-shape parameters; if inputs vary in rank/layout across call sites (e.g. 2D vs 3D activations), Inductor may still trigger guard failures and recompiles for the per-instance compiled function, adding latency spikes at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Log torch._dynamo recompilation counts (TORCH_LOGS=recompiles) during a mixed prefill+decode run for a model using QuantFP8 with custom_ops='none'.

findings.claim: e_score_correction_bias.to(torch.bfloat16) is executed inside apply()/_apply_per_tensor path on every MoE forward. When the bias is not already bfloat16 this materializes a new device tensor and launches a cast op each step. The bias is small ([num_experts]), so the cost is minor, but it is redundant work per forward that could be done once at weight-load time (e.g. in process_weights_after_loading) and cached. If the bias is already bfloat16, .to() is a no-op and there is no cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MoE decode step latency with a non-bfloat16 e_score_correction_bias to measure per-step overhead of the repeated .to(bfloat16) vs a pre-converted cached bias.

findings.claim: Same pattern as trtllm_fp8_moe: e_score_correction_bias.to(torch.bfloat16) is invoked per forward inside the monolithic apply() rather than converted once at load time. Small tensor, so overhead is minor, but it is a repeated cast op every step when the source bias dtype != bfloat16.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure NVFP4 MoE decode step latency with fp32 e_score_correction_bias, comparing per-step .to(bfloat16) against a cached bfloat16 bias.

findings.claim: Auto-selection of FLASHINFER_CUTLASS now additionally requires cutlass_fp4_supported(), and cutlass_scaled_mm_supports_fp4 was tightened to return false unless an SM-specific NVFP4 kernel was compiled in (and false for SM>=130). On GPUs where the NVFP4 kernel is not compiled for the running SM, the auto-selector now falls through to Marlin (weight-only FP4), which is substantially slower for compute-heavy GEMMs than the CUTLASS/FlashInfer FP4 tensor-core path. This is a deliberate robustness trade-off (the previous path would crash), but on affected hardware it manifests as a performance downgrade rather than an error.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM120/SM121 (RTX 50 / DGX Spark) build without the corresponding ENABLE_NVFP4_SM12x kernel, benchmark NVFP4 linear GEMM throughput on the resulting Marlin path vs a build with the compiled CUTLASS FP4 kernel.

findings.claim: flashinfer_cutlass_available no longer requires use_ep, so with VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16=1 the FlashInfer CUTLASS MoE backend can now be selected for pure-TP (non-EP, non-DP) unquantized MoE where it previously fell back to Triton. This is opt-in via env var, but for TP-only configurations the CUTLASS path may perform differently (better or worse depending on shape) than the Triton path it replaces; worth validating it is not a regression for small-batch decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16=1 and TP-only (no EP/DP), compare unquantized MoE decode/prefill throughput on the FlashInfer CUTLASS backend vs the Triton backend for representative token counts.

findings.claim: Adding torch.xpu.empty_cache() inside get_current_memory_usage() forces the XPU caching allocator to release all cached (reserved-but-free) blocks back to the driver on every call. This has two costs: (1) empty_cache() typically triggers a full device synchronization; (2) after freeing cached blocks, subsequent tensor allocations must re-acquire memory from the driver (a slower path than reusing cached blocks). This change mirrors the existing CUDA implementation (cuda.py:206) and is invoked via DeviceMemoryProfiler.current_memory_usage() only at model-load / memory-profiling boundaries (mem_utils.py:51), not on the inference hot path, so the runtime impact is limited to startup/profiling. The intent is to make the reported memory usage accurate (matching CUDA), which is why it is acceptable, but it does add a synchronization + allocator-reset cost each time the profiler measures.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time DeviceMemoryProfiler enter/exit and full memory-profiling pass on XPU before vs. after this change; also measure end-to-end engine startup/warmup time to confirm the added empty_cache() sync does not materially extend init.

findings.claim: If get_current_memory_usage() were ever called repeatedly in a tight loop (e.g. by future profiling/telemetry code), the per-call empty_cache() would repeatedly free and re-allocate driver memory, causing allocator thrashing and reduced steady-state throughput. Currently the only caller is the profiler at load/profile time, so this is speculative, but the coupling of an expensive empty_cache() into a getter named 'get_current_memory_usage' is a latent footgun for future callers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark calling get_current_memory_usage() in a loop with and without empty_cache() to quantify per-call latency and observe reserved-memory oscillation.

findings.claim: Attention._init_turboquant_buffers pre-allocates three fp32 scratch buffers per attention layer, the largest being _tq_mid_o_buf of shape (max_num_seqs, num_heads, tq_max_kv_splits_for_cuda_graph, head_size+1). For a model with B=max_num_seqs=256, num_heads=32, S=32, head_size=128 this is 256*32*32*129*4 bytes ~= 1.35 GB per layer. Multiplied across all attention layers (dozens in typical models) this permanently reserves tens of GB of GPU memory. Because these buffers are registered before the memory profiler runs (as the comment states), they directly shrink the KV cache budget, dramatically reducing achievable batch/context capacity and negating much of the 4x KV compression benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate a ~32-layer model with kv-cache-dtype=turboquant_k3v4_nc and max_num_seqs=256, log total bytes held by _tq_mid_o_buf/_tq_output_buf/_tq_lse_buf across layers and compare available KV cache blocks vs a shared-buffer implementation.

findings.claim: The decode scratch buffers are sized by max_num_seqs (the maximum possible decode batch) for every layer, but decode batches are frequently far smaller. Sizing per-layer by the worst-case batch and never releasing wastes GPU memory that could otherwise hold KV cache blocks. A single shared buffer (one per model, reused across layers) or lazy sizing to the actual decode batch would avoid the per-layer multiplication.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak reserved memory and KV cache block count between per-layer buffers vs a single shared buffer reused across layers, at max_num_seqs=256.

findings.claim: solve_lloyd_max performs Lloyd-Max iteration using a pure-Python trapezoidal integrator (_trapz with n=200 point loops) inside up to 200 iterations, each evaluating 2^bits integrals. This is O(max_iter * n_levels * n) pure-Python work. It is cached via lru_cache(get_centroids) so it runs only once per (d, bits) at startup, but still adds noticeable model-init latency; a vectorized/torch implementation or precomputed table would remove it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time get_centroids(128, 4) cold vs a vectorized torch implementation.

findings.claim: TurboQuant forces flash_attn_version to 2 when a turboquant_ cache dtype is used, overriding FA3. On Hopper (SM90) FlashAttention 3 is materially faster than FA2 for the prefill and boundary (non-quantized) layers, so this override slows the uncompressed-attention portion of the model on supported hardware.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare prefill throughput of boundary/skip layers with FA2 vs FA3 on a Hopper GPU for a model configured with turboquant KV cache.

findings.claim: find_tcmalloc() shells out to `ldconfig -p` and regex-scans its full output during the wheel build (in cmake_build_ext.run). This is build-time only and one-shot, so it has no impact on runtime throughput/latency; it only marginally lengthens the build. Not a runtime perf regression.

Setting os.environ['LD_PRELOAD']=libtcmalloc in check_and_update_config does not affect the memory allocator of the already-running process — LD_PRELOAD is consumed by the dynamic loader only at exec() time. The benefit only materializes for worker subprocesses that are spawned afterwards (VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD='spawn' is set in the same function, so spawned CPU workers inherit the modified environ and will preload tcmalloc). This means the driver/main process itself does not get the tcmalloc allocation speedup, only the workers. This matches the pre-existing libgomp preload pattern, so behavior is consistent, but the perf gain is limited to spawned workers.

The change moves torch thread-limiting from a narrow scope (only InternVL's per-image CPU transform) to wrapping the entire `input_preprocessor.preprocess()` call in `process_inputs()`. Because `OMP_NUM_THREADS` is unset by default, `num_threads` defaults to 1, so ALL input preprocessing for ALL models (text tokenization, multimodal image/audio/video preprocessing that uses torch CPU ops) now runs single-threaded via the process-global `torch.set_num_threads(1)`. On machines with many cores and a single vLLM instance, CPU-bound preprocessing (e.g. large image resize/normalize tensor ops in HF processors) that previously used many intra-op threads will now be serialized to one thread, increasing per-request preprocessing latency and reducing frontend throughput. Previously only InternVL was throttled; now the throttle is applied globally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end preprocessing latency / requests-per-second for a multimodal model with large images (e.g. Qwen2-VL) on a high-core-count host, with OMP_NUM_THREADS unset, before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: `set_default_torch_num_threads` calls `torch.set_num_threads()`, which mutates process-global state rather than thread-local state. `process_inputs()` runs in the frontend/API-server process (called synchronously from `AsyncLLM.add_request`). The context manager reads `torch.get_num_threads()` at entry and restores it at exit. If any other code path in the same process concurrently relies on a higher torch thread count, or if preprocessing is offloaded to worker threads/executors, the global toggle to 1 thread can throttle unrelated torch CPU work and produce non-deterministic contention. The save/restore also adds per-request `set_num_threads` overhead (thread-pool reconfiguration) on every request, including pure-text requests that did not previously touch this code.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark pure-text request throughput (no multimodal) before vs after; also profile torch.set_num_threads call cost under high request rate.

findings.claim: `num_threads` and the `os.environ` lookup / membership check are recomputed on every `process_inputs()` call (per request) rather than being cached once at InputProcessor construction. This is cheap but strictly redundant work on the hot per-request path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark process_inputs at high request rate to measure overhead of repeated os.environ access.

findings.claim: In `transfer_async`, the old code called `expand_block_ids` exactly twice per transfer (once for src, once for dst) to build `src_block_ids`/`dst_block_ids`, then reused those arrays across all tensors with a cheap vectorized `base_ptr + block_ids * bsz`. The new code calls `compute_sub_block_ptrs` `2 * num_tensors` times per transfer, and each call redoes the full sub-block expansion: `np.arange(block_size_factor)`, the broadcasted multiply/add producing a fresh `(num_blocks, block_size_factor)` int64 array, and a `.ravel()` allocation. Since `num_tensors` equals the number of KV cache tensors/layers (commonly 32-80+ for LLMs), the per-transfer CPU work for pointer construction now scales with the layer count instead of being amortized once, adding measurable Python/NumPy overhead on the offload submission path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark transfer_async submission latency for a batch of block mappings with num_tensors in {8,32,80} and block_size_factor>1, comparing per-tensor compute_sub_block_ptrs vs. a single precomputed expansion reused across tensors.

findings.claim: The MADV_POPULATE_WRITE loop in SharedOffloadRegion.__init__ issues one `madvise` syscall per block (`for block in range(num_blocks)`) when a rank is set. For large CPU offload pools (many thousands of blocks) this is thousands of syscalls at initialization, increasing startup/init latency versus a smaller number of coalesced madvise calls over contiguous per-worker regions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time SharedOffloadRegion construction with num_blocks in {1k,10k,100k} and measure madvise loop duration vs a coalesced-range approach.

findings.claim: `_wait_for_file_size` spin-waits with `time.sleep(0.005)` polling `os.fstat` until the creator finishes ftruncate. This is only during worker startup so impact is small, but non-creator workers block for up to a 5ms polling granularity and burn a thread; a filesystem/event-based wait would avoid the poll loop.

The concat of k_nope and k_pe is now routed through a registered custom op (torch.ops.vllm.flashinfer_concat_mla_k) rather than direct tensor slicing. The custom-op dispatch path adds per-call Python/dispatcher overhead (schema resolution, autograd/functionalization wrappers) and an import-inside-function `from flashinfer.concat_ops import concat_mla_k` executed on every call. If the flashinfer kernel is not meaningfully faster than the two slice-assignments for typical prefill token counts, the added dispatch/import cost per attention layer per forward step could offset or exceed the kernel gain. The import is not cached, so it hits the module cache lookup each invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark concat_mla_k custom-op path vs the two-slice fallback for DeepSeek V3 dims (num_heads=128, nope=128, rope=64) across representative token counts (e.g. 1, 128, 2048, 8192), measuring per-call latency including dispatch/import, on the DeepSeek prefill path.

findings.claim: The gating condition only checks self.num_heads==128, but num_heads here is the per-rank head count after tensor-parallel sharding. With TP>1 the local num_heads for DeepSeek V3 (128 total heads) will not equal 128 (e.g. 64 with TP2, 16 with TP8), so the flashinfer kernel path will silently never be taken and the intended speedup is lost precisely in the common multi-GPU deployment scenario, defaulting to the slower fallback with no warning.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the DeepSeek V3 MLA prefill with TP=2/4/8 and assert whether _use_flashinfer_concat_mla_k is True; measure whether the flashinfer path is actually exercised under TP sharding.

findings.claim: set_current_microbatch is called on every microbatch forward step (from forward_step and combined_forward_backward_step). The new implementation adds a second get_attr_wrapped_model(model, 'vision_model', ...) traversal that walks the entire wrapper chain (DDP/Float16Module/etc.) on every microbatch, plus a per-layer Python loop over vision_model.decoder.layers setting layer.current_microbatch. For non-multimodal (LM-only) models this vision lookup is pure wasted work every single microbatch: get_attr_wrapped_model with allow_none=True loops until the attribute is found or the wrapper chain ends, and each miss walks the full module chain. This adds CPU overhead to the hot per-microbatch path that did not exist before. The cost scales with num_microbatches * PP steps and with the depth of the model-wrapper chain.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPU time / step for a pure-GPT (non-multimodal) training run with many microbatches (e.g. 64) before vs after this commit; profile time spent in set_current_microbatch. Also test a multimodal model to confirm the extra per-layer loop cost.

findings.claim: For multimodal models, set_current_microbatch now performs two full wrapper-chain traversals per microbatch (one for 'decoder', one for 'vision_model') and iterates over both decoder and vision layers every microbatch. The vision-layer set is only meaningful when vision CUDA graphs are enabled; when they are not, this is unconditional extra work on the per-microbatch hot path. Consider caching the discovered decoder/vision layer lists on first call or gating the vision lookup behind a config flag so it is skipped when vision cuda graphs are disabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile set_current_microbatch on a LLaVA-style model with vision cuda graphs disabled, comparing steady-state step time before/after.

findings.claim: _wrap_graph_for_vision wraps every captured vision graph in a Python closure that, on each replay call, checks isinstance(result, tuple) and rebuilds a filtered tuple via a generator comprehension. This adds a small per-layer, per-replay Python-level overhead to the vision forward path (executed for every vision layer on every microbatch during training/inference) instead of fixing the output shape at capture time. For deep vision encoders with many layers this is a measurable amount of Python overhead in the forward hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark vision encoder forward with N layers using wrapped vs unwrapped graphed callables to measure per-call Python overhead.

findings.claim: In the fused design, _probabilistic_rejection_kernel recomputes the global log-sum-exp (LSE) for both target and draft distributions inside the per-token acceptance loop (two _compute_global_lse calls per accepted token). The pre-change code precomputed normalized probabilities once via torch.softmax and did a single scalar load per token. Because this kernel is launched with num_warps=1 (32 threads) and iterates serially over draft steps, the added reductions run on a single warp. In practice PADDED_VOCAB_NUM_BLOCKS is small (e.g. 128k/8192 -> next_pow2(16)=16), so per-token cost is a ~16-element reduction repeated per step; this is modest but is strictly more arithmetic per token in the hot rejection loop than the old single-load approach. The trade-off buys a large memory saving (no materialized [num_logits, V] gathered draft logits nor two full softmax tensors), so net it is likely favorable, but the serial num_warps=1 loop now carries extra reduction work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark probabilistic_rejection_sample vs the pre-change implementation across num_reqs x num_speculative_steps x vocab_size (e.g. 32-256 reqs, 1-5 steps, vocab 128k) measuring kernel latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: The block-max/sumexp kernel uses VOCAB_BLOCK_SIZE=8192 while the pre-change gather kernel used BLOCK_SIZE=1024, and the resample kernel uses RESAMPLE_BLOCK_SIZE=1024. The 8192 tile means each program processes 8x more vocab elements with the default num_warps, which increases register pressure/serial work per program and reduces the number of blocks (grid dim) available to hide latency across SMs; for small num_logits this may underutilize the GPU. This is a tuning choice that should be validated rather than assumed optimal.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep VOCAB_BLOCK_SIZE in {1024,2048,4096,8192} for _compute_block_max_and_sumexp_kernel and measure occupancy/latency at representative vocab sizes and num_logits.

findings.claim: This is a bugfix that changes behavior from silently capping cudagraph_capture_sizes to raising a ValueError when max_num_seqs > num_mamba_cache_blocks. The old code (adjust_cudagraph_sizes_for_mamba_cache) capped ALL cudagraph_capture_sizes, which also unintentionally restricted PIECEWISE (prefill) cudagraphs, reducing cudagraph coverage and thus degrading throughput/GPU utilization for prefill batches. The new code removes that over-broad capping (only verifying FULL-decode feasibility via a raise), so it should IMPROVE cudagraph coverage for Mamba/hybrid models rather than degrade it. No net runtime regression is introduced by this change on the happy path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a hybrid Mamba/attention model (e.g. a Bamba/Jamba-style config) with FULL_AND_PIECEWISE cudagraph mode and max_num_seqs <= num_blocks; measure prefill throughput and count of captured PIECEWISE cudagraph sizes before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: In the pre-change behavior, when capture sizes were fully capped away, cudagraphs could be silently disabled (max_cudagraph_capture_size=0) and the engine would still start, running in a slower eager decode path. The new behavior raises instead, so misconfigured deployments will now fail to launch rather than run degraded. This is not a runtime slowdown per se (no valid config becomes slower), but changes failure mode; not a performance regression.

In submodule_moe_forward the two lines that eagerly released tensor references were removed: `node.layer_state.dispatched_probs = None` and `node.layer_state.pre_mlp_layernorm_output = None`. `pre_mlp_layernorm_output` is no longer freed here at all (it is not re-added anywhere in the diff), so its activation memory is now held for the remainder of the layer's lifetime instead of being released right after the experts compute. For MoE layers with large per-token hidden activations this increases peak activation memory. `dispatched_probs` is intentionally kept now (documented as needed by swiglu backward), but the pre_mlp_layernorm_output reference appears to no longer be released early.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak activation memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) for an MoE layer with overlap_moe_expert_parallel_comm enabled, comparing before/after the removal of the pre_mlp_layernorm_output = None release.

findings.claim: should_free_input now returns free_mlp=False for the common AllToAll dispatcher case with num_local_experts==1 (and non-fp8/fp4), whereas previously `mlp` freed its input whenever not HybridEP. This is a correctness fix (the mlp input is needed for GroupedGemm backward when it is identity-passed), but the side effect is that the mlp node no longer eagerly resizes its input storage to 0 in these configurations, so the bf16 mlp input activation now stays resident through backward. This raises peak activation memory for single-local-expert AllToAll MoE runs relative to the prior (buggy) behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak memory for AllToAll MoE with num_local_experts=1, fp8/fp4 disabled, overlap enabled: compare free_mlp True vs False.

findings.claim: backward_dw now wraps the wgrad callables in an explicit `with torch.cuda.stream(self.stream)` context (previously they ran on the ambient/current stream inside only an NVTX range). If self.stream differs from the stream the caller expected, this forces the delayed weight-gradient GEMMs onto the node's dedicated stream, which can change overlap behavior and add cross-stream synchronization/record_stream overhead. This is likely intentional for correctness, but could alter comp/comm overlap in the wgrad phase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare backward wgrad phase timing / stream overlap for delay_wgrad_compute path before vs after forcing torch.cuda.stream(self.stream).

findings.claim: An extra record_stream call was added for shared_expert_output on the comm stream in the mlp-postprocess path. record_stream defers freeing of that tensor's storage until the recorded stream passes the point, which slightly delays memory reclamation of the shared expert output. This is minor and required for correctness when the tensor was created on the comp stream but consumed on the comm stream.

When torch.compiler.is_compiling() is true and the cos_sin_cache dtype/device genuinely differs from the query, _match_cos_sin_cache_dtype now performs the .to() conversion without caching the result back into self.cos_sin_cache (to avoid buffer mutation during tracing). This means the dtype/device cast becomes part of the compiled graph and can be re-executed on every compiled forward pass rather than being amortized once. In practice this only triggers on a real mismatch (the matching case takes the fast early-return path), and it is a deliberate correctness fix for buffer mutation during cudagraph tracing, so the impact is narrow and usually negligible. Inductor may also constant-fold the cast of a fixed buffer. Reported as a speculative note.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark rotary_embedding forward under torch.compile (VLLM_COMPILE) with a query dtype that differs from the cos_sin_cache dtype, comparing per-step latency before/after this change to confirm the cast is not re-run each iteration.

findings.claim: The change moves the tile-scheduler workspace allocation out of the CUDA kernel-launch function (which previously did a per-call cudaMallocAsync + cudaMemsetAsync + cudaFreeAsync) into the PyTorch extension, which now does `at::empty({1}, ...int32)` per call. This is a net performance improvement: it removes the stream-ordered cudaMallocAsync/cudaFreeAsync pair (which serialize with the stream and have non-trivial driver overhead) and replaces it with a fast caching-allocator allocation. The only residual is that a fresh 4-byte tensor is still allocated on every helper invocation rather than reusing a single persistent workspace; for a hot MoE quant path this is a small (caching-allocator) overhead that could be eliminated with a cached/static workspace, but it is negligible relative to the removed cudaMalloc/cudaFree.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark split_quantize_nvfp4 with RHT (all_aligned_token_dim path) over many iterations, comparing per-call latency before/after the change; also compare against a variant that caches the 4-byte workspace tensor.

findings.claim: get_processor now unconditionally calls get_processor_cls_name_from_config(processor_name), which loops over up to three config files (processor_config.json, preprocessor_config.json, tokenizer_config.json). Each call to get_hf_file_to_dict first checks the local cache and, if not found, may trigger a network round-trip via _try_download_from_hf_hub (hf_hub_download). For remote models that lack processor_config.json / preprocessor_config.json, this adds up to 2-3 extra HTTP requests every time get_processor runs for a cache miss. get_hf_file_to_dict is not memoized (no @cache), so nothing dedups these lookups. This adds startup/first-request latency, especially in high-latency network environments. Practically the impact is bounded because get_processor is invoked through cached_get_processor (lru_cache), so it typically runs once per (model,revision,kwargs) tuple; still, the extra file probes are pure overhead compared to the prior AutoProcessor path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time cached_get_processor / get_processor for a fresh (uncached) remote model repo before vs. after this commit, measuring number of HTTP requests and wall-clock time to first processor load.

findings.claim: get_processor_cls_name_from_config always iterates all three candidate config files even after a match could be found, but it does return on first hit, so ordering matters: the most common file (preprocessor_config.json) is checked second. For repos where only tokenizer_config.json carries processor_class, all three probes run. Reordering by likelihood, or short-circuiting when the file list is already known from a single list_repo_files call, would reduce probe count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count get_hf_file_to_dict invocations per get_processor call across a representative set of multimodal model repos.

findings.claim: The grouped MXFP8 path stores TMA tensor descriptors in fixed __device__ global arrays (g_tensor_maps_input/act_input/output_rowwise/output_colwise, each CUtensorMap[64]) and updates them at runtime via a separate update_tma_descriptors kernel using tensormap.replace. This adds an extra device-side kernel launch (and associated synchronization/latency) per group-quantize call before the actual quantization kernel runs. For small grouped tensors (e.g., MoE with many small experts) this per-call fixed launch overhead can dominate, reducing throughput relative to computing descriptors on the host and passing them as __grid_constant__ arguments as the non-grouped mxfp8::quantize path does.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark nvte_group_quantize on many small tensors (e.g., 64 experts x [256,1024] bf16) vs. calling nvte_quantize per tensor in a loop; compare wall-clock and kernel count via nsys.

findings.claim: get_current_tensor_id performs an O(log num_tensors) binary search over offsets_ptr for the VARYING_* shape representations and is invoked from device code to resolve which tensor an offset belongs to. If this is called at fine granularity (per chunk/row inside the kernel) rather than once per CTA, the repeated global-memory reads of offsets_ptr and branch divergence add per-block overhead that scales with number of tensors. The SAME_BOTH_DIMS fast path avoids this via arithmetic, so the varying-shape path is measurably more expensive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare group_quantize throughput for SAME_BOTH_DIMS layout vs VARYING_BOTH_DIMS layout with the same total element count and large num_tensors (e.g., 64).

findings.claim: modify_base_tensor_map copies a full CUtensorMap (128 bytes) into a __shared__ variable per invocation to modify and store it. If invoked from the descriptor-update kernel per tensor descriptor, this uses shared memory and serializes on the shared copy; while modest, using shared memory for the descriptor scratch can lower achievable occupancy of that kernel. Impact is limited to the descriptor-setup kernel, not the main quantization loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the update_tma_descriptors kernel occupancy and duration with nsys/ncu for 64 tensors.

findings.claim: The new diagnostic loop is guarded by `if torch.distributed.get_rank() == 0:` and iterates over all `fsdp_param_groups` calling `logging.info(...)` for buckets that do not fit. Although logging.info is only executed on rank 0 (no collective inside), placing rank-conditional work here is benign for correctness but this loop runs O(num_param_groups) work at construction only. More importantly, the surrounding code (the reworked allocate path) relies on `mem_alloc_context` semantics; the log itself is fine, but running it only on rank 0 makes cross-rank diagnostics inconsistent. Low performance impact, one-time at init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure FixedPoolAllocator construction time with large num_param_groups; negligible vs training step.

findings.claim: When `fallback_to_persistent_buffer=True`, buckets that do not fit the FixedPool now request a persistent tensor from `get_global_memory_buffer().get_tensor(..., name=buffer_name)` with a unique name per bucket (`f"{self.name}_not_fit_in_fixed_pool_{bucket_id}_{size}_{dtype}_{device}"`). Because the buffer name embeds `bucket_id` and `size`, each non-fitting bucket allocates its OWN persistently-held global buffer that is never released (the corresponding `free` path deliberately skips freeing when `fallback_to_persistent_buffer` is True). This trades the previous dynamic (reusable) allocator for a set of permanently-resident buffers, increasing peak GPU memory by the sum of all non-fitting bucket sizes for the entire training run. If many buckets do not fit, this can drive OOM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable fsdp_double_buffer with a model whose largest bucket exceeds the double-buffer size and fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail=True; measure peak allocated GPU memory vs the False (dynamic) path.

findings.claim: In the persistent-buffer fallback branch, the allocation-tracking logic that issues `torch.cuda.synchronize()` on the first/grown allocation of a `(buffer_name, dtype)` now also applies to the per-bucket unique buffer_names created in the fallback path. Since each fallback bucket has a distinct buffer_name, each will trigger at least one full-device `torch.cuda.synchronize()` the first time it is allocated (and again if size grows), serializing the GPU stream. During warmup/first step this adds a device-wide sync per non-fitting bucket, stalling the pipeline and hurting first-iteration latency and overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile first training iteration with several non-fitting buckets and persistent fallback enabled; count torch.cuda.synchronize() calls and measure step-time regression vs fixed-pool-only case.

findings.claim: The `free` method now only delegates to `self.backup_allocator.free(bucket_id)` when `fallback_to_persistent_buffer is False`. When True, buffers obtained from the persistent global memory buffer are intentionally never returned/freed, so their storage stays resident across all iterations. This is the intended design (NCCL UB requires persistent buffers) but it means memory for non-fitting buckets can no longer be reclaimed between phases, unlike the old dynamic-allocator behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track resident memory over many steps with persistent fallback enabled vs disabled.

findings.claim: _prefetch_all_checkpoints launches 8 concurrent background threads that read the rank's entire checkpoint files into the OS page cache while the main loader simultaneously reads (and mmaps/loads) the same files. If the working set exceeds available page-cache RAM (large models), pages prefetched early are evicted before the sequential loader reaches them, so those files must be read from storage again. The result is up to 2x total I/O (once for prefetch, once for the real load) with no cache-hit benefit, which can be slower than not prefetching at all. There is no cap on total prefetched bytes vs. free RAM.

The prefetch runs in a daemon thread concurrently with the main weight-loading loop, and both touch the same files with up to 8 parallel readers. On latency/seek-sensitive or bandwidth-limited storage the concurrent prefetch I/O competes with the actual loader's reads, which can reduce the loader's effective throughput and increase model-init latency instead of decreasing it. Because prefetch uses asyncio.to_thread + a semaphore of 8, it saturates the I/O queue independently of the loader's own read pattern.

Prefetch only warms sorted_files[rank::world_size] (1/world_size of the files), but the main loop iterates over the full sorted_files list, i.e. every rank loads all files. So the prefetch warms only a fraction of the files each rank actually loads, limiting cache-hit benefit; the remaining (world_size-1)/world_size of files are loaded cold. The prefetch slicing appears to assume rank-partitioned loading that does not match the loader's full-list iteration.

Prefetching reads full checkpoint files into the OS page cache, consuming CPU RAM (as also noted in the docstring). On memory-constrained hosts this extra page-cache pressure can evict other useful pages or push the system toward reclaim/swap, indirectly degrading throughput during model init.

flashinfer_trtllm_mxint4_moe hardcodes tune_max_num_tokens=8192 when calling trtllm_mxint4_block_scale_moe. If a batch/sequence produces more than 8192 tokens per MoE invocation (large prefill / high concurrency with big context), the kernel's autotuner/tiling is capped at 8192, likely forcing a fallback to a non-tuned tile config or a suboptimal launch, degrading throughput on large token counts. The cap is not derived from the actual runtime token count or max_num_batched_tokens config.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXINT4 W4A16 MoE forward on SM100 with token counts of 4k, 8k, 16k comparing throughput; verify no perf cliff or fallback at >8192 tokens.

findings.claim: flashinfer_trtllm_mxint4_moe calls e_score_correction_bias.to(torch.bfloat16) and (for DeepSeekV3) router_logits.to(torch.float32) on every forward invocation. The routing bias is a static weight that could be converted once at load time; converting it per-call adds a small H2D-free but GPU kernel + allocation on every decode step, adding minor latency in the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step latency with e_score_correction_bias set, comparing per-call .to(bfloat16) vs pre-converted static bias.

findings.claim: prepare_static_weights_for_trtllm_mxint4_moe loops over experts in Python, calling permute-index computation, gather (advanced indexing), .contiguous(), block_scale_interleave, and convert_to_block_layout per-expert. For models with many experts this is a Python-level loop of many small GPU ops that serialize on the host, increasing model load time. The permute-index cache mitigates recomputation but the per-expert gather/copy work is unavoidable here; it is one-time at load so runtime is unaffected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time process_weights_after_loading for the MXINT4 Flashinfer path vs Marlin path on a model with 128+ experts.

findings.claim: QuarkConfig.maybe_update_config unconditionally calls get_config(model=model_name, config_format="auto", trust_remote_code=False, ...) to re-fetch and re-parse the HF config, even though this config was already loaded during ModelConfig construction and is available via model_config. This adds a redundant config load (potentially a HuggingFace Hub round-trip / file parse) at startup for every Quark-quantized model, whether or not it is DeepSeek/mxfp4. It also hardcodes trust_remote_code=False (contradicting the actual model_config setting), which can cause a failure/fallback for models that require remote code. The cost is one-time at engine init, not per-forward, so throughput is unaffected, but startup latency grows.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine initialization / cold-start time for a Quark-quantized model with and without this patch (should isolate the extra get_config call).

findings.claim: For layers matched by the dynamic mxfp4 path (self_attn projections in DeepSeek), process_weights_after_loading now stores the weight in full precision (params_dtype via ModelWeightParameter) and quantizes it with dynamic_mxfp4_quant(layer.weight) at load time. This transiently materializes the full-precision attention projection weights before quantizing, increasing peak host/GPU memory during model loading relative to the pre-packed uint8 path. This is a one-time load-time cost; runtime memory after loading is the quantized form.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track peak GPU memory during weight loading for a DeepSeek V3 mxfp4 model with the dynamic-quant self_attn path enabled.

findings.claim: get_quant_method now, for every excluded layer whose prefix contains 'self_attn' and when dynamic_mxfp4_quant is enabled, calls get_scheme (which runs _find_matched_config and constructs a QuarkOCP_MX scheme) instead of returning UnquantizedLinearMethod directly. This adds per-layer config-matching work at model build time for attention projections that previously short-circuited. One-time startup cost only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model construction time for a DeepSeek V3 mxfp4 model.

findings.claim: In `use_aot_compile()`, the torch version threshold was raised from `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.10.0.dev")` to `is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev")`, while every other `2.10.0.dev` check in this commit was bumped only to `2.10.0`. This PR adopts torch 2.10.0 as the release version. Previously (on torch reporting >=2.10.0.dev), AOT compile defaulted to ON (default_value="1"); after this change, on torch 2.10.0 the check is False so AOT compile defaults to OFF ("0"). AOT compile lets vLLM load a serialized compiled function directly from disk (see decorators.py __call__: VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE -> torch.compiler.load_compiled_function), bypassing a full Dynamo trace + Inductor recompile at every process/worker startup. Disabling it by default on the release torch means every launch pays the recompilation cost, increasing server warm-start / model-load latency (and per-worker cost under TP/DP). This asymmetry vs. the other version bumps suggests either an intentional gate (2.10.0 AOT considered not-yet-stable, cf. the is_saveable_2_10 workaround) or an inconsistency; either way it changes the default startup-time behavior on 2.10.0.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold vs. warm engine startup / first-forward compile time on torch 2.10.0 with VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE default (0) vs. forced 1, for a representative compiled model (e.g. gpt2 or a small Llama) to quantify the added recompilation latency per launch.

findings.claim: The removed upstream `routing`/`routing_from_bitmatrix` triton_kernels functions are replaced with pure-Python reimplementations `legacy_routing`/`legacy_routing_from_bitmatrix` that build a SparseMatrix, access `mask_metadata` (row/col sorted indices, col_sum), call `make_ragged_tensor_metadata`, and do a `flatten()[combine_indx]` gather on every MoE forward. If the upstream removed functions previously fused some of this metadata construction, this Python-side per-call orchestration could add CPU launch overhead in the routing step on the hot path for GPT-OSS MoE. Functionally it appears equivalent, but it should be verified that no extra device syncs or redundant kernel launches were introduced relative to the previous single `routing` call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark GPT-OSS MoE forward (triton_kernel_moe_forward) routing latency before/after, e.g. via test_shared_fused_moe_routed_transform-style setups, comparing per-call CPU overhead and any added CUDA syncs.

findings.claim: `max_chars_per_token = max(len(tok) for tok in tokenizer_vocab)` adds a full linear scan over the entire vocabulary (often 100K-256K entries) at tokenizer initialization/caching time. Similar scans are added in grok2.py (`max(len(tok) for tok in self._token_to_id)`) and mistral.py (`max(len(tok) for tok in self._vocab)`). This is a one-time startup cost (not per-request), so the impact on runtime throughput/latency is negligible, but it slightly increases model/tokenizer load time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure tokenizer initialization time (get_cached_tokenizer / MistralTokenizer.__init__) with a large vocab (e.g. 256K tokens) before and after the change.

findings.claim: The transfer-parameter construction (`_build_transfer_params`, the per-block loop that builds src_ptrs/dst_ptrs/lengths) was previously executed inside `_sender_worker`, which ran on a 10-thread ThreadPoolExecutor, so parameter building for multiple concurrent requests ran in parallel. After this refactor, `_build_transfer_params` is an `async` method executed on the single `sender_loop` event-loop thread; only the final blocking `batch_transfer_sync_write` is pushed to the executor via `run_in_executor`. All the (potentially non-trivial, per-block, per-request) pointer arithmetic now runs serialized on one thread, reducing effective parallelism of request preparation and adding latency proportional to concurrent request count × blocks/request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefiller with many concurrent remote-decode requests each with large block counts; measure send-preparation latency / P/D handshake throughput vs. parent.

findings.claim: `get_finished()` (called every scheduler step) previously read the finished-sending set with a simple synchronous lock+swap. It now schedules `fetch_finished_sending_reqs` onto the `sender_loop` via `run_coroutine_threadsafe(...).result()` and blocks the caller until the sender event loop picks up and runs the coroutine. If the sender loop is busy (awaiting `sock.recv_multipart`, running param building, or otherwise scheduling other tasks), the hot per-step path stalls on cross-thread coroutine scheduling latency, adding overhead to every step even when nothing finished.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Steady-state P/D run; measure per-step overhead of get_finished() (wall time) with active sender loop vs. parent implementation.

findings.claim: The design spawns `num_sender_tasks = num_workers * 2` (default 20) persistent async worker tasks all awaiting the same `asyncio.Queue`, while the blocking transfer is still bounded to `num_workers` (10) executor threads. The extra tasks cannot increase transfer concurrency beyond the executor size for the blocking `_send_blocks`; they only add per-task scheduling/wakeup overhead on the single event loop. The '2x prevents idling' heuristic in the comment does not apply since the real bottleneck (batch_transfer_sync_write) is executor-thread-bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Vary num_sender_tasks vs num_workers and measure transfer throughput; confirm throughput is bounded by executor thread count.

findings.claim: `start_load_kv` now dispatches `record_send_reqs` via `run_coroutine_threadsafe` to the sender_loop instead of updating `reqs_need_send` synchronously under a lock. `record_send_reqs` mutating `self.reqs_need_send` is fire-and-forget and runs on the sender loop; the state update is no longer synchronous with the scheduler step, so a send may not be visible to the listener immediately, and every start_load_kv incurs cross-thread scheduling. Minor, but adds latency and ordering non-determinism to request registration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure latency from start_load_kv to first block transfer initiation vs. parent.

findings.claim: register_grad_ready() now performs a full Python dict equality comparison (`self.per_param_grad_ready_counts == self.golden_per_param_grad_ready_counts`) on every call, i.e. once per parameter per microbatch, inside the backward hook. This comparison is O(K) in the number of params in the bucket group, and since register_grad_ready is invoked once per param, the total per-bucket-group cost is O(K^2) per iteration. The previous code only checked `len(params_with_grad) == len(params)` which is O(1). For bucket groups containing many parameter tensors this adds non-trivial CPU work on the backward critical path, which can delay the moment the async grad reduce is dispatched and thus erode grad-reduce/compute overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark register_grad_ready() throughput for a bucket group with a large number of params (e.g. 500-2000 param tensors), comparing dict-equality check vs a running counter of matched params; also measure end-to-end backward-hook CPU time and grad-reduce overlap in a large model.

findings.claim: During the first batch, register_grad_ready() no longer dispatches start_grad_sync() (the `if not self.is_first_batch:` guard). Instead the collective is launched from finish_grad_sync(), which runs after the backward pass completes. This means the first training iteration's gradient all-reduce/reduce-scatter does not overlap with backward compute and runs serialized afterwards. The cost is one-time (only the first batch), so impact on steady-state throughput is negligible, but the first step will be slower and this is a behavior regression vs the previous per-bucket overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare wall-clock time of the first training iteration vs subsequent iterations with overlap_grad_reduce=True, before and after this commit.

findings.claim: Two new dicts (golden_per_param_grad_ready_counts and per_param_grad_ready_counts) now hold references to every parameter object in the bucket group as keys, in addition to the existing param_to_bucket map. This is a modest, bounded increase in CPU-side memory (one small-int value per param) and holds param references for the lifetime of the bucket group; negligible for typical models but worth noting.

The removed code path batched the dtype conversion of NVFP4 master_weights into a single torch.cat + .to() + torch.split operation ('single kernel instead of N kernels', per the original comment). After this change, NVFP4 weights now go through the per-weight loop where `master_weight = master_weight.to(model_weight.dtype)` is executed individually for each weight. For a model with N NVFP4 weights this replaces one batched dtype-conversion kernel with N separate small conversion kernels, increasing kernel-launch overhead and reducing GPU utilization during the master-weight quantization step (typically run once per optimizer step / init).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark quantize_master_weights with many (e.g. 100+) small NVFP4 model weights and measure wall-clock time / number of CUDA kernel launches for the dtype-conversion step, comparing per-weight .to() vs the batched cat/convert/split path.

findings.claim: The change removes the explicit `torch._inductor.config.compile_threads = 1` assignment and relies solely on the environment variable `TORCHINDUCTOR_COMPILE_THREADS=1`. These two settings were originally added by two *separate* issues (#10480 for the env var, #10619 for the config assignment), strongly implying the env var alone was found insufficient in some code paths.
