Because `import torch` already runs earlier in this module (before the env var is set), any code path that materializes `torch._inductor.config.compile_threads` (its lazy default = `min(32, os.cpu_count())`) before the env var is consulted will now spawn many parallel Inductor compile worker processes instead of 1. That both increases peak CPU/memory pressure during torch.compile warmup and, in the original bug scenario, can cause hangs/deadlocks (issue #10619). This mainly affects torch.compile / Inductor compilation at engine startup, not steady-state serving.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Start an engine with torch.compile enabled and measure peak process/thread count and RSS during the compile/warmup phase before vs after this commit; also verify compile does not hang on the workload from issue #10619.

findings.claim: The lazy-import gating for `bench` subcommands keys off `sys.argv` (first positional token == 'bench'). This is the intended ~2s import-deferral optimization and is beneficial for `vllm serve`/`vllm --help`, but note that programmatic invocations that construct the parser without a matching sys.argv (e.g. building the parser for help/introspection, or invoking bench through a wrapper that rewrites argv) will not register subclasses and would need to import them another way. No runtime perf regression, but a robustness caveat worth flagging.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time `vllm --help` and `vllm serve --help` cold-start import before vs after the commit to confirm the ~2s saving; verify `vllm bench <type>` still registers all subcommands.

findings.claim: In refresh_metadata, when penalties/step-pooler need token IDs, the code now builds a CPU tensor via _make_prompt_token_ids_cpu_tensor() and then copies it to device. Separately, get_pooling_metadata() also calls _make_prompt_token_ids_cpu_tensor() again whenever any pooling param has requires_token_ids. If both paths are exercised in the same step (a pooling model that also needs penalties, or step-pooler + BOSEOSFilter), the padded [num_reqs, max_prompt_len] CPU tensor is materialized twice with the same per-request Python loop, rather than being cached/shared. For large batches with long prompts this is an O(num_reqs * max_prompt_len) redundant copy each step. Consider caching the CPU tensor within the batch step so it is not recomputed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Pooling model with requires_token_ids=True over a large batch (e.g. 256 reqs, 4k-token prompts); measure per-step get_pooling_metadata / refresh_metadata CPU time vs a variant that caches the CPU token-id tensor.

findings.claim: This is the intended optimization: BOSEOSFilter, SPLADESparsePooler, and GritLMMeanPool previously read pooling_metadata.prompt_token_ids, which was a model-device (GPU) tensor, and performed per-token .item()/indexing operations that forced GPU->CPU synchronizations for every request. Switching to get_prompt_token_ids_cpu() reads from a CPU tensor and removes these syncs, which is the source of the reported throughput gain. No regression here; noting to confirm the CPU tensor is actually populated on the pooling path (it is only built when requires_token_ids is set, which matches these poolers' get_pooling_updates).
findings.suggested_benchmark: BERT/SPLADE/GritLM pooling model E2E throughput with requires_token_ids poolers before vs after; confirm removal of per-request GPU->CPU sync stalls.

findings.claim: BOSEOSFilter now calls get_prompt_token_ids_cpu() unconditionally at the top of forward, which asserts prompt_token_ids_cpu is not None. If bos_token_id and eos_token_id are both -1 (filtering disabled) but the CPU token-id buffer was not populated, this would raise instead of skipping work. Not strictly a perf issue, but an eager unconditional fetch may build/slice the list even when no filtering is needed. Minor.

wvSplitKrc allocates a fresh float32 scratch buffer axl_glbl of shape (N_p2 + N_p2/4) x (M_in + M_in/4) and calls axl_glbl.zero_() on every invocation. For a skinny GEMM used in the latency-sensitive decode path, this adds a device malloc plus a full-buffer memset kernel launch on the critical path each call. The scratch buffer is ~1.25*N_p2 x 1.25*M floats, which is larger than the fp16/bf16 output tensor, so the zeroing is a non-trivial extra memory-bandwidth cost per call. The FAST_UNSAFE_RDC_INIT path that would fold initialization into the kernel is compiled out (set to false), making the explicit zero_() mandatory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark wvSplitKrc vs wvSplitK for skinny decode shapes (M large, N in {16,32,64,128}, K in {2048,4096}) measuring per-call latency; isolate the axl_glbl.zero_() memset cost with/without a persistent preallocated buffer.

findings.claim: In the shipped WVSPLITKRC_1KPASS configuration kFit=512 and kfitsPerRdc=1, so doRdc = (512 < K) is true for any K>512 (the common case). This forces the SplitK reduction to accumulate partial results via global atomicAdd(&glbl[adr], ...) across CUs plus an atomic counter, and only the last shard reads back and converts. Compared to a non-split accumulate, this introduces global-memory atomic contention on glbl plus an extra global round-trip (write partials, atomic counter, read back). For workloads where K is only moderately larger than 512 the split adds reduction overhead without enough K work to amortize it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep K around the 512 boundary (K=512, 1024, 2048) and compare wvSplitKrc latency to the non-atomic wvSplitK path to quantify the atomic-reduction overhead and find the crossover K.

findings.claim: In the non-default (#ifndef WVSPLITKRC_1KPASS) code path, numCuWithFullK is read in the kFit/kfitsPerRdc tuning loop before its declaration (uint32_t numCuWithFullK = ...) later in the function, so it uses an uninitialized value. This would produce incorrect k-split heuristics (bad CU balancing / occupancy) if that path were enabled. It has no runtime effect in the shipped build because WVSPLITKRC_1KPASS is defined, but it is latent and would degrade performance if the multi-pass path is turned on.

When VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER is enabled, the persistent FlashInfer metadata buffers (paged_kv_indptr, paged_kv_indices, paged_kv_last_page_len, paged_kv_indptr_cpu_buffer, etc.) are now allocated in pageable (non-pinned) CPU memory. These buffers are copied to the GPU each step via copy_to_gpu()/copy_() with non_blocking=True. A non_blocking=True H2D copy only truly overlaps with compute when the source is pinned; from pageable memory PyTorch must either fall back to a synchronous copy or route through an internal staging buffer, so the copy effectively blocks the launching thread and cannot overlap with GPU work. paged_kv_indices in particular can be large (max_num_reqs * pages_per_req entries), so per-step attention metadata preparation on the V2 model runner path will incur extra CPU-side stall / reduced H2D overlap on every forward step. This is a per-step overhead in the hot path that trades throughput/latency for the correctness fix described in the commit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-heavy serving benchmark with VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=1 vs =0 on the FlashInfer backend, measuring per-step attention metadata-prep time and end-to-end tokens/s; also profile H2D copy overlap (nsys) for paged_kv_indices/indptr copies with pinned vs pageable source.

findings.claim: torch.repeat_interleave(arange, prompt_lens) is called with prompt_lens being a GPU tensor and no output_size argument. Because the size of the output segment_ids tensor depends on the sum of prompt_lens (a device value), PyTorch must perform a device-to-host synchronization to allocate the output. The previous cumsum+gather implementation had no data-dependent sync (all shapes were statically known), so this introduces a per-forward CPU/GPU stall that can hurt latency and pipeline overlap. prompt_lens_cpu is already available on CPU, so segment_ids/output_size could be computed without syncing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MeanPool.forward with a realistic embedding batch (e.g. 256 seqs, varying prompt lengths, hidden_size 4096) and profile for cudaStreamSynchronize / host-device stalls; compare wall time to the cumsum baseline.

findings.claim: The new implementation replaces a single cumsum + gather with a Python for-loop over token chunks, each launching a separate .to(float32) cast and index_add_ kernel. For large hidden_size (which shrinks chunk_size) and large token counts, this multiplies kernel launches (e.g. hidden_size=8192, 16k tokens -> ~32 chunks -> ~64 launches). Launch and dispatch overhead may offset gains for models with wide hidden dimensions or short sequences where the loop dominates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MeanPool across hidden_size in {384, 1024, 4096, 8192} and total token counts; measure per-call latency vs baseline and count kernel launches via profiler.

findings.claim: index_add_ with duplicated indices (segment_ids maps many tokens to the same segment) uses atomic scatter-adds under the hood, which can be slower than a contiguous reduction like cumsum on some GPUs due to atomic contention when few sequences dominate the batch (e.g., one very long sequence -> heavy atomic contention on its single segment row).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MeanPool with skewed prompt length distributions (one long sequence dominating) to expose index_add_ atomic contention vs uniform-length batches.

findings.claim: For Hopper (SM90) block-FP8 MoE in TP mode (ep_size <= 1), this change forces TRITON to the front of the backend priority list, ahead of DEEPGEMM which otherwise sits earlier in the ordered list. Triton grouped/block-FP8 GEMM on Hopper is typically slower than the DeepGEMM path for larger token counts, so if DEEPGEMM was previously selected for these configs, this could reduce MoE throughput / raise latency for those shapes. The gain (if any) is likely limited to small-batch/decode regimes; large-prefill throughput may regress.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE FP8 block-quant throughput/latency on H100 in TP mode across a range of batch sizes (e.g. 1, 32, 256, 1024 tokens) comparing TRITON vs DEEPGEMM backend selection.

findings.claim: _get_priority_backends rebuilds the backend list and performs list.index/pop/insert reorder operations on every call. This runs only at layer setup/weight-processing time (select_fp8_moe_backend is invoked from layer __init__/process_weights, not per forward), so the cost is negligible and not a runtime hot-path concern.

gather_block_tables() runs every forward step and now reads its source data from host (pinned) memory over PCIe instead of device HBM. block_table_ptrs now points to b.gpu.data_ptr() of UvaBuffer.gpu (a CUDA view of pinned CPU memory), and num_blocks is now a UvaBuffer whose .gpu view also lives in host memory. Previously block_tables and num_blocks were true device tensors. The gather kernel therefore streams the block-table rows for every request across PCIe each decode step; for large max_num_reqs x max_num_blocks tables this is far slower than reading from HBM and can measurably hurt decode step latency / GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step latency of gather_block_tables (and end-to-end decode throughput) with many concurrent requests and long context (large block tables), comparing device-resident block tables vs UVA host-backed block tables.

findings.claim: append_block_ids was changed from a single batched Triton kernel launch covering all requests and all kv-cache groups (with one H2D copy) to a per-request Python call that loops over kv-cache groups doing NumPy slice assignments into the UVA buffer. In update_states this is now invoked once per new/cached request per step, replacing one GPU kernel with O(num_reqs * num_kv_cache_groups) Python/NumPy operations on the hot scheduling path. With hundreds of concurrent requests this adds noticeable CPU-side overhead per step. The author's own TODO ('Too many Numpy invocations. Optimize this.') acknowledges this.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark update_states CPU time per step at high batch sizes (e.g. 256+ running requests, multiple kv-cache groups) before vs after the change.

findings.claim: For each request and group, self.num_blocks.np[i, req_index] is read and written and block_tables[i].np[req_index, ...] is assigned individually. These per-element NumPy indexing operations have high Python overhead relative to the bulk vectorized fill used previously; the single-block fast path (num_new_blocks==1, the common decode case) does one scalar NumPy store per group per request every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark append_block_ids for the decode case (1 new block per request) across many requests/groups.

findings.claim: get_best_data_parallel_rank now always computes a full vectorized score and calls np.lexsort((np.arange(n_ranks), -recency, -scores)) on every routed request, even though only the top-1 rank is needed. The old code returned early on the first matching hash with a cheap dict.get + max(). np.lexsort is an O(n log n) sort that allocates three arrays and only its first element is used (order[0]). Replacing an early-exit argmax with a full sort adds per-request CPU cost in the coordinator's single-threaded ZMQ event loop, which sits on the critical path for request-routing throughput/latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark get_best_data_parallel_rank throughput (requests routed per second) with a warm hash table across varying data_parallel_size (2, 8, 32, 64), comparing np.lexsort vs np.argmax vs the old early-exit dict scan.

findings.claim: _match_vector allocates several NumPy arrays per request (zeros of size n_ranks, and per matching hash: a bool 'present' array, a float 'recency' array, plus two np.fromiter calls). For the typical data-parallel sizes (n_ranks on the order of a few to a few dozen), NumPy operations on such tiny arrays are dominated by Python/NumPy call overhead and are commonly slower than the previous pure-Python dict lookup + max(). This runs once per submitted request in the coordinator's single-threaded routing loop and adds allocation churn (garbage) per request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-request routing latency and allocations (tracemalloc / py-spy) for a stream of requests under LONGEST_PREFIX and FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK policies, comparing the numpy _match_vector path against a scalar Python implementation at small n_ranks.

findings.claim: The refactor removed the early fast-path that returned round-robin when there were no matching hashes. Previously a reverse scan could exit immediately; now even for requests with no cache affinity the code still builds match/recency vectors, computes free_slots/scores, and runs np.lexsort before selecting a rank. This means the extra scoring cost is paid even for cold requests that gain nothing from load-aware scoring beyond round-robin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure routing latency for requests whose hashes never appear in the hash table (all-miss workload) before and after the change.

findings.claim: A new __post_init__ was added to InferenceConfig purely for range validation of prefix_caching_routing_alpha. This is negligible cost but note that if InferenceConfig previously had no __post_init__, dataclass init now runs an extra call per config construction; not a runtime hot path, so impact is minimal.

The per-token-head scale views are cached on the AttentionImpl instance (`_k_scale_cache`/`_v_scale_cache`) and created lazily in `_ensure_scale_caches` from `kv_cache.untyped_storage()` on the first forward. When CUDA graphs are captured (TritonAttentionMetadataBuilder declares AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS, and forward runs eager under piece-wise graphs), the `torch.as_strided` view construction plus `fill_(1.0)` on the scale views is a real GPU op that must run once; if the very first forward inside a captured graph triggers this lazy path, the tensor allocation/`set_()`/`fill_` are illegal during graph capture and can hang or corrupt. Even outside capture, the guard `if self._k_scale_cache is not None: return` means the views bind to whatever kv_cache tensor was passed on the first call; if the KV cache tensor is later reallocated/moved the stale storage view silently reads wrong memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable CUDA graph full-decode mode with cache_dtype=int8_per_token_head and run a decode workload; compare correctness/latency vs eager.

findings.claim: The new per-token-head cache-write kernel `_reshape_cache_per_token_head` uses grid (num_tokens, NUM_KV_HEADS) with one program per (token, head), where each program only processes a single head of HEAD_SIZE_PADDED elements. Compared to the original `reshape_and_cache_kernel_flash` which tiles up to TILE_SIZE=2048 elements across all heads with num_warps=16, this new kernel launches num_kv_heads times more programs, each doing a tiny absmax reduction over one head. During prefill (large num_tokens) this multiplies kernel-launch/scheduling overhead and yields poor occupancy per program, slowing the KV-cache write path relative to the fp8 per-tensor path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark reshape_and_cache throughput for a prefill batch (e.g. 8k tokens, 8 kv heads, head_size 128) with int8_per_token_head vs fp8.

findings.claim: get_kv_cache_shape pads head_size by scale_pad = sizeof(float32)/sizeof(cache_dtype) elements per head (4 for int8, 4 for fp8/uint8). This inflates the KV cache page size by scale_pad/head_size per head (e.g. +4/128 ~= 3.1% for head_size 128) even though only ONE float32 scale per (token,head) is needed but scale_pad elements are reserved contiguously with the head. More importantly for small head_size the padding is a large fraction, reducing the number of KV blocks that fit in GPU memory and thus effective batch/context capacity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compute num_gpu_blocks with int8_per_token_head vs plain int8 for head_size 64 and 128 and compare max concurrency.

findings.claim: Per-token-head dequant requires loading a per-(block,slot,head) float32 scale and multiplying inside the attention tiles, replacing the single scalar `tl.load(k_scale)` used by the per-tensor fp8 path. This adds an extra strided float32 load and broadcast-multiply per tile in the hot attention loop; for long-context decode this is measurable extra memory traffic and ALU compared to per-tensor fp8.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark unified_attention decode latency at 32k context with int8_per_token_head vs fp8 per-tensor.

findings.claim: fused_rope_kvcache_supported() now returns False for per-token-head quant, disabling the fused ROPE+KV-cache-update path (rocm_aiter) for these dtypes. On ROCm this forces separate rope and cache-write kernels, adding kernel launches and an extra pass over K/V versus the fused path used for other dtypes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On ROCm with aiter enabled, compare decode step latency for int8_per_token_head (fused disabled) vs fp8 (fused enabled).

findings.category: redundant fill on scale cache init
findings.claim: `_ensure_scale_caches` calls `.fill_(1.0)` on both k and v scale strided views on first use. These are strided (non-contiguous) writes over the entire KV cache scale region, an O(num_blocks*block_size*num_kv_heads) GPU op executed once at first forward; harmless in steady state but adds a one-time strided-write cost that scales with total KV cache size (potentially GBs of strided access).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-forward latency with a large KV cache (many blocks) for int8_per_token_head.

findings.claim: In the new _update_intermediate_metadata, when intermediate_offsets_gpu is provided, intermediate_counts_gpu.tolist() is called every step to compute per_request_intermediate_counts and total. This forces a GPU->CPU synchronization on the hot prefill path for every prefill batch, even though the rest of the extraction was carefully vectorized to be CUDA-graph/sync-free. The .tolist() sync stalls the CPU on the pending GPU work and can reduce prefill throughput, partially undermining the stated goal of removing .item()/host sync from this path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill-heavy dynamic inference on a hybrid Mamba model with prefix caching enabled; measure per-step latency and tokens/sec with vs without the .tolist() sync (e.g., replace with an async count buffer).

findings.claim: MambaMetadata.__init__ now unconditionally allocates three GPU buffers of size MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST*max_requests (chunk indices int64, abs positions int32) plus a conv_gather_offsets tensor. These are allocated for all hybrid inference contexts regardless of whether prefix caching / intermediate extraction is actually used. For large max_requests this is a small but permanent increase in device memory footprint that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU memory of a hybrid inference context init across a range of max_requests with prefix caching disabled, before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: get_intermediate_gpu_data() and _update_intermediate_metadata are now called on every prefill step whenever mamba_slot_allocator is not None, whereas previously intermediate handling was gated behind requires_mamba_state_extraction / get_intermediate_offsets() returning non-None. This adds unconditional vectorized GPU work (cumsum, repeat_interleave, masked gather, buffer fills) to every prefill step, including the common case where no request needs intermediate extraction, increasing per-step CPU launch overhead and kernel count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare prefill step latency on a hybrid model with prefix caching enabled but no cache hits (no intermediate offsets) before vs after the change.

findings.claim: The use_triton_conv1d config option and the per-step _use_triton_conv1d_this_step selection were removed. Previously, on non-CUDA-graph steps a user could opt into the Triton varlen conv1d kernel to avoid the per-request causal_conv1d_fn loop (which launches a variable number of kernels with .item() calls). Removing the option forces the per-request loop path for eager (non-graph) prefill, which can be slower for batches with many prefill requests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Eager-mode prefill with many concurrent prefill requests on a hybrid model; compare conv1d stage time using per-request causal_conv1d_fn vs Triton varlen kernel.

findings.claim: The new default disables XPU graph capture (sets cudagraph_mode = CUDAGraphMode.NONE) whenever VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH is unset, which is the default. Previously, on supported PyTorch and single-DP setups, XPU graph capture (typically PIECEWISE for FLASH_ATTN) would be used. Running without graph capture means the decode path executes per-op in eager mode, incurring host-side kernel launch overhead on every layer/step. For small-batch decode-bound workloads (where launch overhead dominates), this measurably increases per-step latency and reduces GPU utilization. This is a deliberate correctness/stability tradeoff, but it is a real default performance regression for all XPU users who do not set the new env var.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput/latency (tokens/s, per-step latency) on a single Intel XPU for a small-batch LLM decode workload with VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=0 (new default) vs =1 (prior behavior with PIECEWISE graph).

findings.claim: In Float8BlockwiseQTensor.fsdp_post_all_gather, columnwise data is no longer all-gathered but instead derived locally on every all-gather via out._create_columnwise(), which runs a tex.fp8_transpose kernel plus a scale-inv transpose/copy over the FULL unsharded (gathered) tensor. This trades halved communication volume for extra per-iteration GPU compute + a newly allocated columnwise buffer on every forward/backward gather. When network bandwidth is not the bottleneck, or when the previous code already had the transposed columnwise data ready, this can be a net slowdown and increases peak GPU memory (the fp8_transpose output buffer is allocated fresh each call since out._columnwise_data was set to None). The transpose+copy is O(full_M*K) per gather.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP2 forward+backward step time and peak memory for a Float8BlockScaling (2D) linear layer, comparing all-gather-both vs all-gather-rowwise+local-transpose across various hidden sizes and interconnect bandwidths (NVLink vs. cross-node).

findings.claim: In fsdp_post_all_gather, out._create_columnwise() is called when columnwise_usage is True, and then out.update_usage(rowwise_usage, columnwise_usage) is called immediately after. If columnwise_usage is True and rowwise_usage is False (backward pass with reshard_after_forward), update_usage will set _rowwise_data/_rowwise_scale_inv to None after they were just used to build the columnwise data. This is correct but means the freshly all-gathered rowwise buffers are discarded; fine functionally, but _create_columnwise + then dropping rowwise is more work than gathering columnwise directly would have been in the columnwise-only case. Also update_usage(rowwise=True, columnwise=True) would itself call _create_columnwise, so the explicit preceding _create_columnwise() call is partially redundant for the both-usage path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fsdp_post_all_gather CPU/GPU time for the both-usage (reshard_after_forward=False) path to check for double transpose work.

findings.claim: The FSDP2 private-API import `from torch.distributed.fsdp._fully_shard._fsdp_common import TrainingState` was moved inside fsdp_pre_all_gather (executed on every all-gather) instead of at module scope like float8_tensor.py/mxfp8_tensor.py. A cached module import is cheap, but performing an import statement inside a hot per-parameter, per-iteration path adds a small dict lookup and function-call overhead on each gather. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPU overhead of fsdp_pre_all_gather over many iterations with the import inside vs. at module scope.

findings.claim: The commit moves `_init_kv_cache_quant(self, quant_config, prefix)` and the assignment of `self.kv_cache_dtype`/`self.calculate_kv_scales` to AFTER `get_attn_backend(...)` and after the impl backend-name checks. `get_attn_backend` is called with the local `kv_cache_dtype` (which may be `fp8`), then the fp8->fp8_ds_mla conversion mutates `cache_config.cache_dtype`. This is init-time (per-layer) work only, so there is negligible steady-state performance impact; the concern is correctness of what `kv_cache_dtype` the backend was selected with vs. what the impl is later given. No runtime throughput/latency regression is introduced by the reordering itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Model load / engine init time for a DeepSeek-V3.2 model with --kv-cache-dtype fp8 across FLASHINFER_MLA_SPARSE and FLASHMLA_SPARSE backends; verify no double backend selection.

findings.claim: Setting `self.supports_quant_query_input = True` for FLASHINFER_MLA_SPARSE enables an extra per-forward FP8 quantization of the query (via `_DecodeConcatQuantFP8`) whenever fp8 kv-cache is used. This is required by the TRTLLM-GEN kernel (mixed bf16+fp8 not supported), so it is a necessary cost of the fp8 path, but it adds a query-quantization kernel launch on every decode step that did not exist before for this backend. The offsetting benefit is a smaller/faster fp8 attention kernel and reduced KV-cache memory bandwidth; net effect should be positive for large context but adds fixed per-step overhead for small batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput and per-step latency for FLASHINFER_MLA_SPARSE with fp8 vs bf16 kv-cache at small (1-4) and large (256+) batch sizes; measure the added query-quant kernel time via nsys.

findings.claim: For FLASHMLA_SPARSE the code now silently rewrites a user-requested `fp8`/`fp8_e4m3` kv-cache to the `fp8_ds_mla` (656-byte) format. The fp8_ds_mla format uses 656 bytes/token vs a plain fp8 layout, so users expecting standard fp8 memory savings will instead get the ds_mla layout. This is a memory-footprint / expectation difference rather than a regression versus the parent (parent forced fp8_ds_mla too via config.py), and is only a suggestion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-token KV cache byte size and total GPU KV memory for FLASHMLA_SPARSE fp8_ds_mla vs FLASHINFER_MLA_SPARSE plain fp8 at fixed max_model_len.

findings.claim: Every `enqueue()` on the writer side now calls `self._spin_condition.notify()`, which issues a zmq PUB `send(b"\x00")` on the local notify socket. Previously the reader/writer synchronization was purely lock-free shared-memory + `sched_yield()` with no per-message syscall on the write path. This adds a zmq send (a syscall / lock into the zmq I/O thread) to every broadcast of a SchedulerOutput, i.e. once per model step per local reader group. Under high request throughput (many steps/sec, TP>1) this is extra per-step latency on the critical scheduling->worker broadcast path. The SNDHWM=1 + CONFLATE mitigations reduce backpressure/queuing but do not remove the send() call itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step broadcast latency and end-to-end decode throughput at high QPS with TP>1 comparing acquire_write/enqueue with vs. without the notify() send on the hot path.

findings.claim: The reader now busy-loops (sched_yield) for `busy_loop_s=1` second after the last read, then blocks on `poller.poll()`. When the reader is idle beyond 1s and a new message arrives, it depends on the PUB->SUB notification arriving to wake it. With zmq PUB/SUB slow-joiner semantics and CONFLATE, an initial notification after an idle period can be dropped or delayed, adding wake-up latency (up to the warning-interval poll timeout) on the first request after idle. Previously the reader kept polling shared memory at least via sched_yield or the 0.1s sleep, guaranteeing bounded detection latency without relying on socket delivery. The default busy loop was also reduced from 3.0s to 1.0s, shortening the always-fast window.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-token latency of a request arriving after the engine has been idle for >1s, comparing this change to the old SpinTimer/SpinSleepTimer behavior.

findings.claim: Each local reader now creates three additional zmq sockets (SUB notify socket, PAIR write_cancel_socket, PAIR read_cancel_socket) plus a Poller, in addition to the existing local_socket. For deployments with many MessageQueues / many local readers this increases zmq socket and file-descriptor count and associated memory/context overhead versus the previous timer-only approach.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count fds and RSS of a worker process with the new SpinCondition sockets vs. the old SpinTimer implementation across a large number of MessageQueue instances.

findings.claim: `build_one_prefill_chunk` in indexer.py now unconditionally computes `token_to_seq` on every metadata build: `torch.arange(...)` on CPU + `torch.repeat_interleave(seq_idx, seq_lens_cpu[reqs_start:reqs_end])` producing a tensor of size sum(seq_lens) (up to max_prefill_buffer_size, which for DeepSeek-V3.2 can be many hundreds of thousands of entries), followed by a synchronous `.to(self.device)` H2D copy. This is done once per prefill chunk per step. If the new `token_to_seq` field is not actually consumed by the sparse indexer op path in this commit (the CUDA/ROCm sparse_attn_indexer functions do not reference it), this is wasted compute + an extra host-to-device copy on the per-step critical path that can add latency during prefill.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step prefill metadata build latency for DeepSeek-V3.2 sparse indexer with long contexts (large max_prefill_buffer_size), comparing wall-clock of build() before/after adding token_to_seq.

findings.claim: `_aiter_ops.py` now performs a module-level import of `rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer` / `_fake` from `vllm.v1.attention.ops.rocm_aiter_mla_sparse` at the top of the file. That module in turn imports triton (`from vllm.triton_utils import tl, triton`) and aiter-related paths, pulling heavier attention-op modules into the import graph of a broadly-imported utility module. This can increase process startup/import time and risks import-order/circular-import cost even on non-ROCm platforms where the op is never used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `python -c 'import vllm._aiter_ops'` cold import time before/after the change on a CUDA-only build.

findings.claim: The sparse indexer path now routes through a CustomOp layer (`SparseAttnIndexer`) whose `forward_native` dispatches to `forward_cuda`/`forward_hip` at every call, adding an extra Python indirection layer (attribute lookups + branch on `current_platform.is_cuda()/is_rocm()`) per indexer invocation compared to the previous direct `torch.ops.vllm.sparse_attn_indexer(...)` call. On decode steps this runs per token-batch and the added per-call Python overhead is small but non-zero.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode-step latency for the indexer op via CustomOp vs direct torch.ops call to quantify Python dispatch overhead.

findings.claim: For the q_len_per_req > 1 (MTP/spec-decode) path the pre-allocated zero-init buffer is NOT used (out= is skipped), and instead the code executes `o[num_real:] = 0` on the kernel's freshly-allocated output on every call. This means the stated optimization ('avoid a memset kernel on every CUDA graph replay') is only realized for single-token decode (q_len==1); for q_len>1 the code still incurs both a fresh allocation inside the kernel and an explicit memset every replay. The memset is issued unconditionally whenever num_real < o.shape[0], adding a small kernel launch + write on each decode step of that path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MTP/speculative decode (q_len_per_req>1) with FlashInfer MLA backend under CUDA graph, measure per-step decode latency with vs. without the o[num_real:] = 0 zeroing.

findings.claim: The `_decode_out` buffer grows monotonically (condition only checks `shape[0] < B` and never shrinks) and is held for the lifetime of the attention impl object. With one impl instance per attention layer, this persistently reserves B*num_heads*kv_lora_rank * dtype_size bytes per layer for the decode-only path even during prefill-heavy phases, a small persistent memory increase relative to the prior transient new_empty allocation. Same pattern in cutlass_mla.py _decode_out.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure steady-state GPU memory of a many-layer MLA model (e.g. DeepSeek) before/after this change during decode.

findings.claim: With `prevent_retokenization` defaulting to True, every chat completion request that contains a prior assistant message performs a SECOND full `tokenizer.apply_chat_template(...)` (Jinja template render + tokenization) over the prefix (messages up to the last assistant message). This doubles the (CPU-bound, per-request) chat-template + tokenization work in the request-handling hot path for multi-turn conversations, increasing request latency and CPU load. The first call already tokenizes the full message list; the added call re-renders/re-tokenizes a large prefix just to align prefix tokens.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request latency of /v1/chat/completions with a multi-turn conversation (>=1 prior assistant message) with prevent_retokenization True vs False; compare apply_chat_template CPU time.

findings.claim: The new `logger.warning("Generation log probs is None ...", json.dumps(result, indent=4))` eagerly serializes the entire `result` dict (which includes full generated_tokens, log_probs, top-n logprobs, routing_indices, etc.) with pretty-printing on every choice where generation_log_probs is None. json.dumps(indent=4) over large result payloads is expensive and blocks the async request handler. Although generation_log_probs defaults to [] (rarely None), when it does trigger it adds a large, avoidable serialization cost per choice.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trigger the None-logprobs branch with a large generated sequence and measure the json.dumps(indent=4) cost per choice.

findings.claim: `warnings.warn("Avoiding prefix retokenization...")` is invoked inside the request handler on every request when prevent_retokenization is True (the default). Even though Python dedups identical warnings by default, warnings.warn still acquires the warnings registry lock and does per-call filter processing, adding minor overhead on the hot request path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark request throughput with vs without the warnings.warn call in the hot path.

findings.claim: `_sanitize_messages_for_template` and `_sanitize_tools_for_template` build full deep-ish copies (dict(message), dict(call), dict(function), dict(tool)) of every message/tool_call/tool on each request instead of the previous in-place normalization. For long multi-turn histories with many tool calls this adds per-request allocation/copy overhead, though it is CPU-only and typically small relative to tokenization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark request handling with long conversations containing many tool_calls, comparing old in-place normalization vs new copy-based sanitization.

findings.claim: In _RegisterDelayedWgradForExperts.backward, after launching backward_dw on the dedicated wgrad_stream and recording the event, the code immediately calls torch.cuda.current_stream().wait_event(event). This forces the main backward stream to block until the delayed expert wgrad GEMM has completed before proceeding. If the subsequent backward work that is supposed to overlap with the wgrad (the A2A combine backprop) is issued on the current stream, this synchronization can serialize wgrad and the following backward instead of overlapping them, defeating the intended overlap and adding latency. The wait should be placed only at the point where the wgrad results are actually needed, not immediately after recording.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE training step with overlap_dispatch_backward_with_experts_wgrad enabled vs disabled; measure per-iteration backward time and NCCL/A2A vs GEMM overlap in nsys.

findings.claim: _process_post_backward_gradients now applies two additional list comprehensions over param_list on every backward gradient-processing call, each invoking getattr(p,'skip_backward_post_hook') and hasattr(p,'post_wgrad_grad_acc_hook') per parameter. For non-MoE or delay-disabled runs these attributes are never set, so this is pure per-parameter Python overhead added to a hot backward path proportional to number of params processed per bucket.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward step time for a dense (non-MoE) FSDP model before vs after the change to detect added Python overhead in the grad-processing hook.

findings.claim: setup_delayed_wgrad_for_dispatch_backward_overlap creates a distinct torch.cuda.Stream per MoELayer when overlap_dispatch_backward_with_experts_wgrad is enabled. With many MoE layers this allocates many separate CUDA streams; using many concurrent streams can increase scheduling overhead and reduce effective overlap versus a single shared wgrad stream. This is a one-time allocation but scales with layer count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare a deep MoE model (many layers) with per-layer wgrad streams vs a single shared wgrad stream for backward throughput and GPU utilization.

findings.claim: _RegisterDelayedWgradForExperts.backward iterates over module.parameters() (all MoELayer params: router, shared experts, latent projections, experts) on every backward to find those with post_wgrad_grad_acc_hook, even though only expert grouped-GEMM params carry that hook. For layers with large parameter counts this adds per-parameter Python iteration to the backward critical path each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward time for MoE layer with delayed wgrad enabled, profiling Python-side time spent in _RegisterDelayedWgradForExperts.backward.

findings.claim: In reshape_and_cache_flash_kernel, the branch condition changed from `if (is_contiguous_heads)` to `if (is_contiguous_heads && kv_scale_stride == 0)`. This means that when per-attention-head scales are used (kv_scale_stride != 0) with an NHD (contiguous-heads) cache layout, the kernel now falls into the `else` (HND) path. That path processes the KV row head-by-head, assigning only 32 threads (one warp) per head and doing a per-head `vectorize_with_alignment` of length `head_size` instead of a single vectorized copy of the full contiguous row of `n_elems = num_heads*head_size`. For NHD layout this is strictly less efficient: it forfeits the coalesced full-row vector copy and instead serializes over heads with reduced parallelism (fewer active threads per head, warp-loop over heads). Any model using per-head FP8 KV quant with NHD layout pays a reshape_and_cache slowdown compared to a design that vectorizes the contiguous row and only indexes the scale per head.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark reshape_and_cache_flash with NHD layout, per-head scales ([num_heads]) vs per-tensor scales ([1]) for realistic num_heads/head_size/num_tokens, comparing kernel time.

findings.claim: The HND branch now recomputes `k_scale_val`/`v_scale_val` and reconstructs CopyWithScaleOp inside the per-head loop for every head. In the per-tensor case (kv_scale_stride==0) this reads the same scalar `k_scale[0]`/`v_scale[0]` and rebuilds the op on each of the `num_heads` iterations rather than hoisting it out of the loop as the parent code did. This adds a small redundant per-head global-memory load and op construction in the HND per-tensor path. Impact is minor relative to the copy itself but is a strict regression vs the previous hoisted computation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark reshape_and_cache_flash with HND layout, per-tensor scale, large num_heads, comparing against parent commit kernel time.

findings.claim: For per-head query quantization, self.query_quant is now built with GroupShape(-1, block_size) instead of PER_TENSOR. Grouped (per-group/block) static FP8 quantization of the query is generally more expensive than a single per-tensor scale quantization (more scale broadcasts / less-fusible kernel), adding per-decode-step overhead in the query quant path for per-head-quantized models. Confidence is moderate because this path only activates for the new per-head feature and depends on the scaled_fp8_quant group implementation cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode-step latency of QuantFP8 static per-tensor vs GroupShape(-1, block_size) on query tensors of typical [num_tokens, num_heads*head_size] shape.

findings.claim: The new `draft_logits` buffer allocated in RequestState has shape [max_num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, vocab_size] in model dtype. For a typical config (max_num_reqs=256, num_speculative_steps=5, vocab_size=128000, fp16) this is ~328 MB of persistent GPU memory reserved for the entire server lifetime when probabilistic rejection sampling is enabled (rejection_sample_method='probabilistic'). This directly reduces the KV-cache budget and thus max batch size / concurrency. The config docstring itself acknowledges 'more memory to cache draft logits'.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory / usable KV-cache blocks with rejection_sample_method='strict' vs 'probabilistic' at fixed max_num_reqs and vocab_size.

findings.claim: In model_runner._sample, the probabilistic path passes `self.req_states.draft_logits[input_batch.idx_mapping]` which performs an advanced-index gather copying [num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, vocab_size] logits into a new contiguous tensor on every decode step. For 256 reqs x 5 steps x 128000 vocab x 2 bytes this materializes hundreds of MB per step and adds a large memory-bandwidth-bound copy in the sampling hot path. Indexing the whole draft_logits buffer per step is a significant per-step overhead compared to the strict path (a small Triton kernel over token IDs).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step sampler latency (decode step time) for strict vs probabilistic rejection sampling at large batch size.

findings.claim: probabilistic_rejection_sample computes full-vocab softmax over both target_logits ([num_logits, V]) and draft_logits ([num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, V]) every decode step, then runs residual-logit and resample kernels over the full vocab. These full-vocab elementwise passes are additional memory-bandwidth-bound work not present in strict rejection sampling, materially increasing spec-decode sampling cost per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile softmax + residual kernels in probabilistic_rejection_sample vs strict_rejection_sample kernel at vocab_size=128k, num_reqs=256.

findings.claim: The Eagle draft-token gumbel_sample calls now always pass processed_logits_out=draft_logits_out[:, step] when caching is enabled, causing the gumbel kernel to write the full temperature-applied logits ([num_reqs, vocab_size]) to global memory for every speculative step. This adds a full-vocab store per step per draft token that did not exist before, increasing draft-generation bandwidth cost even before rejection sampling runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure draft-generation (generate_draft) latency with cache_draft_logits=True vs False at large batch and vocab size.

findings.claim: The refactored Sampler.__call__ now computes `input_batch.positions[input_batch.logits_indices]` and `input_batch.input_ids[input_batch.logits_indices]` internally on every sampler call. Previously the caller could reuse `sample_pos`/`input_ids` already gathered. This creates two extra index gathers per sampler invocation; minor but repeated on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark sampler call overhead before/after refactor at typical decode batch sizes.

findings.claim: The new `preserve_intragpu_slots` postprocessing runs on the host: it copies phy2log, replicas_idx, and old_global_expert_indices from GPU to CPU (three `.cpu().numpy()` transfers) and then executes nested Python loops (`for gpu_idx in range(num_ranks)` x `for slot_idx in range(slots_per_gpu)`, plus a `for layer_idx in range(num_layers)` fill pass). For large deployments (e.g. 32 ranks, ~9 slots/GPU, ~58 layers) this adds a few thousand Python-loop iterations plus device->host copies to each rebalance. This cost is intended to be amortized by avoiding unnecessary expert-weight copies for experts that stay on the same GPU, but if `num_ranks`/`slots_per_gpu` grow the host-side loop could become a non-trivial fraction of rearrangement latency. Because rebalancing is infrequent, the net effect is likely a win, but the scalar Python loops are a latent scaling concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time rebalance_experts() end-to-end (with and without old_global_expert_indices) for large ep sizes (e.g. 32-64 ranks, 58 layers) and measure the added CPU time of preserve_intragpu_slots vs. the reduced GPU copy time.

findings.claim: In move_to_workspace, `new_physical_to_logical_map[layer].cpu().numpy()` is called even though new_physical_to_logical_map was already moved to CPU in advance (the `.cpu()` at the async setup path). The extra `.cpu()` on an already-host tensor is a redundant copy per transferred layer; harmless but avoidable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark move_to_workspace over all layers, measuring time spent in the .cpu().numpy() conversion when the tensor is already on host.

findings.category: PDL / GPU overlap optimization not effective
findings.claim: The stated goal of this change is to prefetch the LoRA weight tensor `b` before the GDC (programmatic dependent launch) barrier, so that the weight load overlaps with the tail of the primary kernel — mirroring the established pattern in `mm_k` (kernel_utils.py), which does `tl.load(b_ptr)` then `if USE_GDC: gdc_wait()` then `tl.load(a_ptr)` inside the K loop. However, the diff only removed the *comment* above the pre-loop wait, not the pre-loop `gdc_wait()` itself. As a result the kernel now performs `gdc_wait()` BEFORE the K loop (the retained `if USE_GDC and not IS_PRIMARY: tl.extra.cuda.gdc_wait()` right after the accumulator init) AND again inside the loop after the first `b` load. Because the barrier is already satisfied by the pre-loop wait, the in-loop wait is redundant and the intended prefetch/overlap of the weight load with the primary kernel's tail is not achieved. The optimization is effectively a no-op; the pre-loop `gdc_wait()` should have been removed so the first `b` prefetch executes before the barrier.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark fused_moe_lora shrink+expand on a PDL-capable GPU (e.g. Hopper) comparing this commit vs. a variant that removes the pre-loop gdc_wait; measure expand-kernel latency / end-to-end MoE-LoRA decode throughput to confirm whether the intended overlap materializes.

findings.claim: The newly added `gdc_wait()` is placed inside the K loop and thus is emitted/evaluated on every one of the grid_k iterations rather than once. While a GDC wait that is already satisfied is cheap, issuing the intrinsic every iteration adds unnecessary instructions to the hot inner loop; the reference pattern relies on the barrier being needed only on the first iteration. Confirm the compiler hoists/no-ops the redundant waits so there is no added per-iteration cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Inspect generated PTX/SASS for the expand kernel to verify the in-loop gdc_wait is only materialized once, and benchmark expand-kernel latency vs. a single-wait variant.

findings.claim: When Mamba prefix caching is enabled (mamba_cache_mode != "none") and async scheduling was left at its auto-enable default, async scheduling is now force-disabled. Async scheduling overlaps CPU-side scheduling with GPU execution to hide scheduling latency and reduce inter-step GPU idle bubbles; disabling it (synchronous scheduling) measurably reduces decode throughput and increases per-step latency for Mamba models using prefix caching. This is a deliberate correctness trade-off (async scheduling is documented as incompatible with Mamba prefix caching), not an accidental regression, and the config check itself runs once at startup with negligible cost. Users who rely on async scheduling with these models will see lower throughput than before enabling prefix caching.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode throughput (tokens/s) and per-step latency for a Mamba/hybrid model (e.g. Qwen3-Next) with prefix caching enabled, comparing async_scheduling forced on (pre-fix behavior) vs off (post-fix), under a steady decode workload.

findings.claim: The added warmup code (two torch.distributed.barrier() calls plus an all_reduce on the data_parallel_group) is inserted in the nccl_ub branch of _ParamAndGradBuffer construction. This block runs once per buffer being registered. If multiple param/grad buffers are created (e.g., one per bucket group / model chunk / dtype), this pays two collective barriers + an all-reduce per buffer during initialization. Since barriers are global synchronization points that force all ranks to wait for the slowest, this adds initialization latency at startup. It is a one-time cost (not per-step), so it does not affect steady-state training throughput, but it can noticeably lengthen startup time on large clusters where barriers are expensive and the buffer registration happens many times.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure DDP/distributed-optimizer initialization wall-clock time with nccl_ub enabled, on a large node count, comparing before/after this commit; also count how many times the nccl_ub registration path is entered per process.

findings.claim: Two full torch.distributed.barrier() calls surround the warmup all_reduce. The trailing barrier() after the warmup all_reduce is likely redundant: the all_reduce itself is a synchronizing collective on the same group, so the second barrier mainly guarantees CPU-side ordering rather than providing needed correctness. On large groups each barrier is a global sync that stalls until the slowest rank arrives, so the pair of barriers adds avoidable initialization latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the init path with both barriers vs. one barrier vs. barrier-free warmup to quantify the barrier cost at scale.

findings.claim: In the new apply_weights, layer.weight_packed.t() is computed on every forward pass rather than once at load time. .t() itself is a metadata-only transpose view (no data copy), so the direct cost is negligible; however, if the underlying _xpu_C.int4_gemm_w4a16 kernel requires or internally materializes a contiguous/transposed layout, this could introduce a per-call copy of the packed weight. Compared with the previous implementation, which transposed and made the weight contiguous once in process_weights_after_loading, pre-transposing the packed weight at load time and storing it would be safer and avoids any risk of per-forward layout work. As written, if the kernel handles the strided view natively, there is no measurable regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark a wNa16 Linear forward on XPU (e.g. Qwen3-4B-GPTQ-Int4) comparing per-call latency with weight_packed transposed at load vs. per-call .t(), to confirm the kernel does not materialize the transpose each call.

findings.claim: The commit removes the shared_experts from the modular kernel (setting m_fused_moe_fn.shared_experts = None, or not passing self.base_layer.shared_experts to FusedMoEModularKernel) with the intent that 'the runner overlaps them with routed experts via a separate CUDA stream.' The change in fused_moe.py alone does not implement any dual-stream overlap; it only stops the kernel from computing the shared experts inline. If the corresponding runner-side dual-stream logic is not present/enabled on the path that constructs this modular kernel for LoRA, the shared experts would either not be computed at all (correctness) or be computed serially without overlap, negating the claimed 1.07x throughput gain. Reviewer should verify the runner-side overlap path is actually reached for the FusedMoEWithLoRA construction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a MoE model with shared experts + LoRA (e.g. a DeepSeek/Qwen-MoE variant) and measure end-to-end output-tokens/s with and without this change; also verify the separate-CUDA-stream overlap actually happens via profiler traces.

findings.claim: Overlapping shared experts on a separate CUDA stream competes for the same SMs as the routed-expert GEMMs. On small-batch/decode workloads where the routed-expert kernels already saturate the GPU, splitting the shared-expert work onto a second stream can introduce launch/synchronization overhead and reduce per-kernel occupancy rather than improving throughput. The 1.07x gain is workload-dependent and may regress for decode-heavy or low-concurrency serving.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode-only (batch size 1-8) vs prefill-heavy workloads for a shared-expert MoE + LoRA model, comparing latency/throughput before and after the change.

findings.claim: This commit reverts PR #37205, removing the specialized gpt-oss min-latency router GEMM kernel (Tier 2 in GateLinear.forward). For gpt-oss MoE models on SM90+/Hopper/Blackwell with bf16 weights and supported dims (num_experts in {32,128}, hidden_size 2880), the router GEMM now falls back to Tier 3 (cuBLAS bf16->fp32) when out_dtype==fp32, or Tier 4 (F.linear via ReplicatedLinear) otherwise. The reverted kernel was purpose-built for the decode/min-latency regime (batch <= 128): a tall-skinny GEMM (M=num_experts, N=tokens=1..128, K=2880) with TMA bulk loads and mbarrier multi-stage double buffering and programmatic-stream-serialization launch. cuBLAS / F.linear are generally less efficient than a hand-tuned kernel on these small-batch latency-bound shapes, so the per-layer router GEMM (executed every decode step for every MoE layer) will incur higher latency. This is a throughput/latency regression for gpt-oss decode inference on those platforms.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_router_gemm.py (pre-revert) for openai/gpt-oss-20b/120b comparing the specialized kernel vs F.linear/cuBLAS across batch sizes 1..128; and end-to-end decode latency (ITL/TPOT) for gpt-oss on H100/B200 before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: With Tier 2 removed, gpt-oss routers whose out_dtype is not fp32 now hit Tier 4 (F.linear). Tier 4 may add an extra dtype cast of x to weight.dtype and an output cast to out_dtype (the `x.to(...)` / `output.to(...)` conversions), introducing additional elementwise-cast kernels per router call that the specialized kernel folded into a single fused GEMM+bias. This is a minor extra memory-traffic cost per decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile GateLinear.forward for gpt-oss decode with out_dtype != fp32, counting extra cast kernels (nvprof/nsys) before vs after revert.

findings.claim: In Qwen3OmniMoeAudioEncoder.forward, multiple CPU/GPU synchronization points are introduced per audio-encoder invocation: chunk_num.sum() implicitly materialized when building chunk_lengths, chunk_lengths.tolist(), feature_lens_after_cnn.max().item(), and aftercnn_lens.tolist(). Each .item()/.tolist() forces a device-to-host sync that stalls the CUDA stream. Since these run on every multimodal request that contains audio, they can add avoidable latency, though the encoder itself is heavy so the relative cost is modest. Some of this (e.g. chunk_lengths construction with a Python list and per-element indexing assignment) also runs on CPU.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile audio_tower.forward latency on a batch of audio inputs, counting cudaStreamSynchronize/device syncs via torch profiler; compare against the transformers flash_attention_2 implementation it replaces.

findings.claim: cu_seqlens is built with a Python-level loop over aftercnn_lens.tolist(), doing per-chunk list.extend/append. For inputs with many audio windows this scales with the number of chunks on the CPU and creates a large Python list before converting to a tensor, adding host-side overhead that is not overlapped with GPU work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPU time of the cu_chunk_lens construction loop for long audio (many windows) inputs.

findings.claim: The encoder computes max_seqlen once via compute_attn_mask_seqlen (a .max() producing a tensor), which is then passed through every layer's MMEncoderAttention and converted with .item() inside the flash-attn wrapper. Because the custom op flash_attn_maxseqlen_wrapper calls max_seqlen.item() internally, and the encoder passes the same tensor to every layer, each of the encoder_layers triggers a device-to-host sync inside flash_attn per layer. With config.encoder_layers layers this is one sync per layer per forward pass, serializing GPU execution. Pre-materializing max_seqlen to a Python int once before the layer loop would avoid the repeated syncs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: torch profiler trace of the layer loop counting cudaStreamSynchronize calls; compare passing max_seqlen as int vs tensor.

findings.claim: The added `block_table.clamp_(min=0)` runs an in-place elementwise CUDA kernel over the decode block_table (shape [num_decodes, max_num_blocks_per_seq]) on every `build()` call for the decode path. This is an extra GPU kernel launch per forward step. For long-context / large max_model_len the block_table can be wide (e.g. tens of thousands of blocks per row), so the clamp touches a non-trivial number of int32 entries each step. It is small relative to the attention kernels, but is nonetheless a new per-step cost that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode-step metadata build latency (build()) before/after for a batch with large max_num_blocks_per_seq, e.g. seq_len=163840, block_size=64, batch=128.

findings.claim: `block_table = common_attn_metadata.block_table_tensor[:num_decodes, ...]` is a view (slice) into the runner's persistent block_table device tensor, and `clamp_(min=0)` mutates it in place. This permanently overwrites the -1 padding sentinels in the shared block_table buffer with 0. If the same block_table tensor is reused/read elsewhere (e.g. other attention groups, or logic that distinguishes padded rows via -1), the mutation persists across the step and could force those consumers into extra handling or lose the ability to short-circuit padded requests, indirectly affecting work done. This is a correctness-adjacent concern, but the in-place mutation of a shared persistent buffer is a hidden cost/side effect that should be scoped (e.g. clamp only occurs under cudagraph padding).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify no other consumer reads -1 sentinels from the same block_table buffer within a step; measure whether alternative masked-write paths get triggered.

findings.claim: The parent config file defined dedicated, autotuned configs for three H100 platform keys (nvidia_h100_pcie, nvidia_h100_sxm5, nvidia_h100), each with tuned entries such as pid_type=persistent_blocked, num_stages=3, num_warps=32 for intermediate_4096. This commit removes all three H100 platform sections and folds them into a single nvidia_h200 section keyed by num_tokens. On H100 GPUs the silu_mul_fp8 Helion kernel will no longer find a platform-specific config and will fall back to the generic 'default' config (num_warps=8, num_stages=2, tensor_descriptor indexing) or trigger re-autotuning. The default is measurably worse than the previously tuned H100 entries (which used persistent_blocked scheduling and deeper pipelining), so the fused SiLU+mul+FP8-quant kernel will run slower per launch on H100 hardware. Because this kernel is on the MLP activation hot path, throughput on H100 deployments regresses.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the silu_mul_fp8 Helion kernel microbenchmark on an H100 (pcie and sxm5) at intermediate_size in {2048,4096} and batch=256, comparing per-call latency before vs after this commit (i.e., tuned H100 config vs default fallback).

findings.claim: The number of config keys per platform grew from ~3 (intermediate_2048/4096 + default) to ~40+ (cross product of intermediate_size and num_tokens buckets). pick_silu_mul_fp8_config performs a linear scan over config_keys with per-key string splitting and int parsing on every kernel invocation. With the much larger key set this per-call host-side selection cost grows proportionally. It remains small (microseconds) relative to a GPU kernel launch, so impact is minor, but it is now run on a longer list each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-time pick_silu_mul_fp8_config with ~40 keys vs ~3 keys over many iterations to confirm the added host-side selection overhead is negligible.

findings.claim: The receiver (P1) cache key is changed from `feature.identifier` (LoRA-prefixed, e.g. `loraName:mm_hash`) to the un-prefixed `feature.mm_hash`, while the P0 sender caches (MultiModalProcessorSenderCache / ShmObjectStoreSenderCache) still key by the LoRA-prefixed `identifier` (produced by `_get_mm_identifier`). This breaks the documented P0/P1 key-mirroring invariant in `BaseMultiModalCache`. When the same base image is used with two different LoRAs (A then B), P0 keyed by `B:H` misses and therefore re-sends the FULL processed tensor over IPC to P1, even though P1 already has it cached under `H`. Under vision-LoRA workloads that reuse images across LoRAs, this causes repeated large-tensor IPC transfers (and, for the SHM path, repeated re-serialization/put attempts) that the cache was meant to avoid — added latency and IPC/serialization overhead on the hot input path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With mm_processor_cache_gb>0 and enable_tower_connector_lora=True, send N requests reusing the same set of images across 2+ LoRA adapters; measure per-request input-processing latency and IPC bytes transferred P0->P1, comparing to the same images with a single LoRA.

findings.claim: Because P0 still keys by the LoRA-prefixed identifier while P1 now keys by the base mm_hash, the P0 sender cache will hold one entry per (lora, mm_hash) pair whereas P1 holds one entry per mm_hash. The two caches no longer track the same number of live items, so P0's LRU eviction/size accounting (which is intended to mirror P1) will diverge: P0 may evict/re-fetch under the assumption that P1 evicts in lockstep, potentially causing occasional cache misses on items P1 still holds (or vice versa), leading to extra recomputation/IPC. This is a secondary effect of the same key mismatch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fill the P0 sender cache near capacity using multiple LoRAs over a shared image set and measure the effective hit rate reported by make_stats() versus actual P1 cache contents over a long request stream.

findings.claim: MiniCPMMoE.forward now calls fused_experts(..., inplace=False) instead of inplace=True. With inplace=True, fused_experts_impl reuses `hidden_states` for its output (out_hidden_states = hidden_states); with inplace=False it does `torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`, allocating a new [num_tokens, hidden_size] output tensor on every MoE forward pass. For MiniCPM-MoE this adds one extra activation-sized allocation per layer per step, increasing peak memory and adding an allocator/copy cost. Unlike the default_moe_runner change (which drops a clone because disable_inplace is asserted), this MoE path uses fused_experts directly and has no shared-experts stream, so there is no correctness reason forcing out-of-place here — the change appears to be a defensive/consistency edit that costs a redundant buffer. The residual add downstream (hidden_states * scale) does not require the MoE input to be preserved, so inplace would still be safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MiniCPM-MoE decode/prefill throughput and peak-memory benchmark comparing inplace=True vs inplace=False in MiniCPMMoE.forward; measure tokens/s and torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated per step at various batch sizes.

findings.claim: compute_maxsim_scores pads every pair in a mini-batch up to the batch-wide max_q and max_d and then runs a single dense torch.bmm of shape (batch_size, max_q, max_d, dim). Because pairs are batched strictly in input order with no length-based grouping/sorting, a single long query or document in a batch inflates max_q/max_d for all pairs, so total FLOPs can substantially exceed the sum of the per-pair q_len*d_len*dim work done by the old per-pair compute_maxsim_score. When sequence lengths within a batch are highly skewed, the wasted padded compute can offset or exceed the kernel-launch savings the batching is meant to provide.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Score a batch with highly skewed query/doc lengths (e.g. one pair with 512 tokens, rest with 8) and compare throughput vs the per-pair path.

findings.claim: For the common single-doc-per-step case (batch_size == 1), the batched path still allocates q_mask/d_mask boolean tensors, builds padded q_batch/d_batch via a Python copy loop, performs masked_fill_ with -inf on the full token_scores tensor, and calls unbind — none of which are needed when there is exactly one pair with no padding. This adds constant per-call overhead and extra kernel launches compared to the previous simple torch.matmul(q,d.T).amax(-1).sum() path, which can slightly hurt latency in low-batch scoring workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark postprocess_pooler_output latency scoring one document per step (batch_size=1) before vs after this change.

findings.claim: The padded tensors are filled with a Python for-loop over the batch, each iteration issuing an individual slice assignment q_batch[i, :q_len] = q_emb.to(...) / d_batch[i, :d_len] = .... For large batch_size this serializes many small copy/cast ops on the host, adding per-item Python and kernel-launch overhead that scales linearly with batch size and is not overlapped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark compute_maxsim_scores with max_batch_size=64 many short sequences and measure the fraction of time spent in the fill loop vs bmm.

findings.claim: The commit removes CUDA stream priorities from both offloading handlers. Previously the CPU->GPU load handler used high-priority streams (priority=-1) and the GPU->CPU offload handler used low-priority streams (priority=1). Load transfers (CPU->GPU) are on the critical path because a request cannot begin computing until its KV blocks are loaded, whereas offload transfers (GPU->CPU) are off the critical path. By dropping priorities, all transfer streams now compete at the default priority against each other and against compute-adjacent work, so the GPU scheduler no longer preferentially advances load work over background offload work. Under concurrent load+offload traffic this can increase load latency (and thus time-to-first-token / stall newly scheduled requests) even though the new wait_stream() fix reduces offload interference with sampling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the offloading connector under a workload that simultaneously loads and offloads KV blocks (mixed prefill-from-CPU + decode-offload), and measure per-request load-transfer completion latency / TTFT with and without stream priorities.

findings.claim: For the GPU->CPU direction each offload transfer now issues stream.wait_stream(torch.cuda.current_stream()) at submission time. This makes the offload stream wait for ALL work currently enqueued on the current (compute) stream, not just the KV writes it depends on. If the current stream has substantial unrelated pending work, the offload transfer can be serialized behind it, delaying when offload actually starts and lengthening how long GPU KV blocks must be pinned/held. This is intentional (the bugfix), but the coarse whole-stream wait may add more serialization than strictly necessary versus waiting on a specific event.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode-step throughput and offload transfer start-latency with the wait_stream call vs a targeted event wait on only the layers being offloaded.

findings.claim: Store jobs are now deferred: prepare_store_kv only appends (job_id, transfer_spec) to _unsubmitted_store_jobs, and actual transfer_async submission happens at the start of the NEXT engine step in start_kv_transfers. This intentionally moves offload off the token-generation critical path, but it also delays the actual GPU->CPU copy by up to one full engine step, meaning GPU KV blocks that are being offloaded stay resident (and pinned against reuse until the store completes) slightly longer. In steps where no subsequent start_load_kv is invoked promptly, offload initiation is postponed, marginally increasing peak GPU KV memory pressure.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under sustained decode with heavy offloading, measure peak GPU KV block occupancy and offload completion latency before vs after deferral.

findings.claim: By converting FusedRMSNormGated into a CustomOp, the default dispatch behavior changes. In vLLM's default Inductor compilation mode, `custom_ops` defaults to "none", so `CustomOp.enabled()` returns False and `dispatch_forward` returns `forward_native` (with compile_native=False, so it is NOT wrapped in torch.compile). Previously the module always executed the single fused Triton `rms_norm_gated` kernel. Now, whenever the enclosing model forward is NOT under an active Inductor graph (eager execution, enforce_eager, profiling runs, or if the call site is inside an opaque custom op), the o_norm computation runs as a long chain of individual eager elementwise CUDA kernels (x.float(), pow, mean, rsqrt, multiplies, sigmoid, .to(dtype)) instead of one fused Triton kernel. This is a measurable per-token latency/launch-overhead regression for KDA (Kimi Delta Attention) and any other user of FusedRMSNormGated in the eager path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark FusedRMSNormGated on realistic decode shapes (e.g. num_tokens in {64,128,512}, head_dim=128, activation='sigmoid') comparing forward_cuda (Triton) vs uncompiled forward_native in eager mode; measure per-call latency and CUDA kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: forward_native operates on the original (unflattened) input shape rather than reshaping to 2D as the previous rms_norm_gated did. When compiled by Inductor as part of the model graph, multi-dimensional / variable leading-dim inputs (KDA passes shape [1, num_tokens, num_heads, head_dim]) may trigger extra recompiles or less-optimal fusion versus the previous 2D-normalized kernel path. This is speculative and depends on dynamic-shape handling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run KDA model under Inductor with varying num_tokens and count dynamo recompiles / compare compiled forward_native latency vs the previous Triton path.

findings.claim: forward_native falls back to forward_cuda (the Triton kernel) whenever residual is not None or prenorm is True. Under Inductor with custom ops disabled, this mixes a compiled/native fast path with an opaque Triton kernel; for models that exercise the residual/prenorm path there is no torch.compile fusion benefit and the added dispatch/branch is pure overhead vs. the prior single unconditional Triton kernel. Impact is minor and only affects the residual path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark forward with residual!=None comparing new dispatch (forward_native -> forward_cuda fallback) against the original direct rms_norm_gated call.

findings.claim: The explicit KV repeat_interleave that was previously done once before the per-sequence loop is replaced by passing enable_gqa=True into scaled_dot_product_attention, which is now invoked once per sequence inside the loop. On GPU fused SDPA backends this is a clear win (KV heads are broadcast without materialization). On CPU, PyTorch's SDPA GQA handling is not always fused; if the internal implementation expands/repeats KV per call, that expansion now happens once per loop iteration (per sequence) rather than a single time up front. Worst case this matches previous cost; it is unlikely to regress but the per-iteration invocation of the GQA broadcast could be slightly less efficient than a single up-front repeat for workloads with many short sequences. Speculative and version-dependent on the CPU SDPA kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run CPU attention (CPUAttentionBackendImpl._run_sdpa_forward) with a GQA config (num_heads > num_kv_heads) on a batch of many short prefill sequences; compare wall-clock time before/after this change on the same PyTorch version.

findings.claim: This change is a performance improvement, not a regression: it adds an extra guard (self.drafter.supports_mm_inputs) so that _gather_mm_embeddings — which gathers multimodal embeddings from the target model's encoder cache — is skipped during the draft proposal step when the draft model is text-only. Previously it ran the (potentially non-trivial) MM embedding gather every draft step even though the text-only drafter cannot consume the result, wasting CPU/GPU work. No slowdown is introduced by the diff itself; the only marginal cost is one extra attribute read per step, which is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run speculative decoding with a multimodal target model and a text-only draft (e.g. EAGLE) and compare per-step latency / throughput before vs after; measure time spent in _gather_mm_embeddings.

findings.claim: add_dummy_requests_for_expert_parallel_step now computes token_to_local_position via torch.cumsum, torch.repeat_interleave, torch.arange and torch.remainder instead of the previous single arange+modulo. This introduces several extra small GPU kernel launches per EP-dummy step. Because this is a per-step host->GPU sequence of tiny ops (some depending on a preceding .item()-free tensor result but still forcing multiple kernel launches), it adds launch overhead on the decode critical path for EP ranks that have no real requests. Magnitude is small (dummy step), so this is a minor concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark add_dummy_requests_for_expert_parallel_step latency vs parent for a decode-only EP configuration; measure host launch overhead per step.

findings.claim: is_decode_only() now branches on _using_cuda_graph_this_step and reads padded_batch_dimensions.prefill_req_count when CUDA graphs are active. This is a correctness/behavioral change but not a direct perf regression; however if _using_cuda_graph_this_step is stale or padded_batch_dimensions reports a non-zero prefill count for what would otherwise be a decode step, the attention path could take the more expensive (prefill/mixed) branch in attention.py instead of the fast decode kernel, slowing that step. Low confidence without runtime state.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step decode latency with CUDA graphs enabled + speculative tokens before/after; verify decode-only steps still hit the fast decode kernel branch.

findings.claim: Additional slicing of request_log_probs and top_n_logprobs on the finish/trim path. These are small per-request slices only executed when a request overshoots num_tokens_to_generate, so overhead is negligible; noted only for completeness.

The decode path now unconditionally reshapes q into (num_requests, tokens_per_request, H, D) and reshapes output back to (B*S,1,H,D) for every decode step, even when tokens_per_request==1 (non-speculative decode). reshape on contiguous tensors is typically a metadata-only view (no copy), so overhead should be near-zero, but the extra Python-level ops and the num_requests = seqlens_k.shape[0] / division add a small per-layer host overhead on the hot decode path. If any reshape triggers a copy (non-contiguous q), it would add a memory copy per layer per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark per-token decode latency (non-speculative, num_speculative_tokens=0) before/after to confirm the added reshapes introduce no measurable per-layer overhead or hidden copies.

findings.claim: launch_gpt_oss_router_gemm() re-encodes two CUtensorMaps (cuTensorMapEncodeTiled) and calls cudaFuncSetAttribute(cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize) on every single invocation of the router GEMM. cuTensorMapEncodeTiled is a relatively expensive host-side driver call and cudaFuncSetAttribute is redundant to call each launch (it only needs to be set once per kernel). The router GEMM runs once per MoE layer per forward step (i.e. many times per decode step across dozens of layers), so this per-call host overhead adds measurable CPU-side latency to the critical decode path, especially at small batch sizes where the kernel itself is tiny and launch overhead dominates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark end-to-end host latency of gpt_oss_router_gemm at batch_size=1..16 vs. the F.linear fallback, measuring CPU launch overhead (not just GPU kernel time) across many repeated calls to expose per-call tensormap encoding cost.

findings.claim: cudaFuncSetAttribute for MaxDynamicSharedMemorySize is issued on every launch instead of once (e.g. via a std::call_once / static flag). This is a driver call in the hot path that could be hoisted out to one-time initialization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare host-side call latency with vs. without per-call cudaFuncSetAttribute over N=10000 repeated launches.

findings.claim: cudaLaunchConfig_t config is stack-allocated and only some fields (gridDim, blockDim, dynamicSmemBytes, stream, attrs, numAttrs) are set; config.numAttrs and attrs[0] are initialized, but if the struct has other fields left uninitialized this is fragile.
