Not a direct perf regression but could cause undefined launch behavior. Low confidence this affects performance.

The kernel writes output in transposed/column-major fashion (output[tn * M + tm]), i.e. output is laid out as [N, M] indexed by expert-major. If downstream consumers of the router logits expect a [batch, num_experts] row-major layout, an extra transpose/copy may be introduced on the Python side to fix the layout, adding a kernel and memory traffic. Cannot confirm without seeing the changed gate_linear dispatch; flagged as speculative.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify the output tensor layout expected by MLPBlock router consumer and check whether an extra .t()/.contiguous() is inserted for gpt-oss path.

findings.claim: The WvPrGrp (waves-per-workgroup) template argument for the wvSplitKQ dispatch is reduced across the board (16->12 for N=1/2, 16->8 for N=3, 16->4 for N=4). This directly reduces the number of active waves per workgroup, cutting parallelism and memory-fetch throughput within each CU. Especially for N=4 the drop from 16 to 4 waves is a 4x reduction in intra-block parallelism; for large-K skinny GEMMs (activation columns per wave) this leaves compute/memory units idle unless the kernel body was restructured to compensate. This is a plausible net throughput regression for the N=3/4 batched decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark ops.wvSplitKQ for N in {1,2,3,4} across large K (e.g. 2048, 4096, 28672) on MI300/MI355 comparing pre/post commit latency.

findings.claim: A new unconditional `asm volatile("s_waitcnt vmcnt(0)")` is inserted before __syncthreads() in the LDS-prefetch loop of both wvSplitKQ_hf_sml_ and wvSplitKQ_hf_. On non-gfx950 targets the LDS load path still uses ordinary vector loads (`*((bigType*)&s[k]) = *((bigType*)&A[k])`) which already imply a vmcnt dependency at consumption; forcing a full vmcnt(0) barrier for every workgroup stalls the wave until ALL outstanding vector memory ops complete, serializing the prefetch and adding latency each kernel invocation. On non-gfx950 architectures this is pure overhead versus the previous code which had no explicit waitcnt.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile wvSplitKQ prefetch phase on gfx942 (MI300) with/without the s_waitcnt to measure added stall cycles.

findings.claim: A new local `scalar_t biases[N][YTILE]` array is materialized and populated (with an unconditional zero-init `= {}`) even in the common no-bias case; while only thread 0 executes it, it adds register/stack pressure and an extra initialization loop compared to the previous inline conditional load. Minor, but affects the epilogue of every invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare register usage (rocprof/llvm-mca) and epilogue latency with BIAS==nullptr before/after.

findings.claim: The B-matrix fetch removed the `if (k_ >= K) break;` early-out and now always issues loadnt with clamped indices `min__(k_, K - A_CHUNK)` and `min__(y+m, M-1)*Kbp`. This turns previously-skipped out-of-range iterations into real (redundant/overlapping) global loads of B, increasing memory traffic for tail iterations where K is not a multiple of THRDS*A_CHUNK*UNRL. For skinny GEMMs that are memory-bound this adds wasted DRAM bandwidth on tail blocks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark wvSplitKQ with K not a multiple of the unroll tile (e.g. K=2048+16) vs aligned K to quantify extra B loads.

findings.claim: apply_write() now calls async_tensor_h2d(self._staged_write_contents, ..., pin_memory=True) on every invocation. This allocates a fresh pinned (page-locked) host buffer per call via torch.tensor(list, pin_memory=True). Pinned allocation is significantly more expensive than pageable allocation (cudaHostAlloc page-locks memory and, unless served from the caching host allocator, involves kernel-level page pinning). Previously write_contents was a pre-allocated, reusable UvaBufferPool that was only (re)allocated on rare growth events (guarded by a comment noting reallocations are 'infrequent in practice'). apply_write is on the per-step hot path (called for every block table, mrope positions, token ids, etc. via apply_staged_writes), so this converts a near-zero-cost buffer reuse into a per-step pinned allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark StagedWriteTensor.apply_write() in a tight loop with typical staged content sizes, comparing per-call latency and host-allocator activity before vs after; also end-to-end decode-step throughput with large batch/many block tables.

findings.claim: Previously write_contents used a UVA-backed buffer (get_accelerator_view_from_cpu_tensor), so the Triton _apply_write_kernel read the staged contents directly from the zero-copy UVA view with no explicit device buffer allocation or H2D memcpy. async_tensor_h2d instead materializes a CPU tensor and does t.to(device, non_blocking=True), which allocates a new GPU tensor and issues a real host-to-device copy every step. This adds a device allocation plus a genuine H2D transfer per apply_write that did not exist before, increasing PCIe traffic and GPU-side work on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile (nsys) a decode step to compare number/size of H2D memcpys and cudaMalloc events for write_contents before vs after; measure per-step wall time.

findings.claim: async_tensor_h2d builds the tensor directly from a Python list via torch.tensor(data, ...), whereas the old UvaBufferPool.copy_to_uva path assigned into a preallocated numpy/pinned buffer (dst[:n] = x). torch.tensor over a Python list of ints/floats has per-element Python-object conversion overhead that scales with content length and occurs every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time torch.tensor(list, pin_memory=True) vs numpy-buffer assignment for representative content lengths.

findings.claim: The pre-change code computed `is_eager_execution = compilation_config == CUDAGraphMode.NONE`, comparing the whole CompilationConfig object against an enum member, which is always False. As a result, the AITER RMS norm custom op ('+rms_norm') was NEVER enabled based on the intended condition (`use_aiter_rms_norm and not is_eager_execution`), because `not is_eager_execution` was always True and the enable path would fire regardless of graph mode — but more importantly the guard 'perform best when CUDA Graph capture is enabled' was effectively broken. The new code fixes this to `compilation_config.cudagraph_mode == CUDAGraphMode.NONE`, correctly gating the custom-op enablement. This is a positive behavioral change but it changes which kernels are selected at runtime (AITER RMSNorm custom op now enabled only under the intended cudagraph conditions), directly affecting kernel dispatch and thus RMSNorm throughput on ROCm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On ROCm/MI300X with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_RMSNORM enabled, compare RMSNorm layer throughput and end-to-end decode latency before/after, under both eager (cudagraph_mode=NONE) and PIECEWISE/FULL cudagraph modes, verifying the '+rms_norm' custom op is now dispatched only in non-eager modes.

findings.claim: `apply_config_platform_defaults` is now called earlier in VllmConfig.__post_init__ (right after the generic quant_fp8 handling at ~line 810) rather than inside `check_and_update_config` (invoked much later at ~line 990). Moving the AITER custom-op population before the compilation-mode/cudagraph_mode selection means the custom_ops list is used to derive fusion-pass decisions (enable_norm_fusion/enable_act_fusion, which key off is_custom_op_enabled('rms_norm')/'quant_fp8'). If the timing of when custom_ops are populated relative to when pass_config resolves its callable defaults changes, the set of enabled Inductor fusion passes could change, altering compiled-graph fusion and thus kernel performance. Worth verifying the fusion passes see the same custom_ops set as before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On ROCm with AITER enabled at -O2, dump compilation_config.pass_config resolved flags (fuse_norm_quant, fuse_act_quant) and the final custom_ops list before/after this commit; confirm no unintended change in which fusion passes are enabled, and benchmark compiled decode throughput.

findings.claim: `compilation_config.custom_ops.append('+sparse_attn_indexer')` is unconditionally appended in the new apply_config_platform_defaults, same as before. No perf regression here, but note it is now executed at an earlier config stage; if apply_config_platform_defaults could be invoked more than once (e.g., config re-initialization / copy paths), the unconditional append (no membership guard) would accumulate duplicate '+sparse_attn_indexer' entries, growing the list and repeatedly re-registering the op. Confirm this hook is invoked exactly once per config.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate/copy VllmConfig multiple times on ROCm and assert custom_ops contains a single '+sparse_attn_indexer' entry; measure any config-init overhead if invoked repeatedly.

findings.claim: The new TMA-aligned strided scale allocation in per_token_group_quant_fp8 (column_major_scales + tma_aligned_scales) uses torch.empty_strided with stride (1, tma_aligned_m) for the 2D case. The scale tensor is then written by the existing _per_token_group_quant_fp8_colmajor Triton kernel, which was written assuming the previous permute()-based layout where the leading (padding) dimension stride was exactly M (not the TMA-aligned round-up). If the kernel is not also updated to receive/use the new column stride (tma_aligned_m rather than M), scale values will be written to wrong offsets, producing incorrect quantization and forcing an accuracy fallback or reprocessing. This is a functional/perf risk given the kernel launch in the parent passes x_s.stride(1) as y_s_col_stride.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_per_token_group_quant_fp8 with tma_aligned_scales=True across M=1,7,8,83,84 and verify scale.stride()[-1]==get_tma_aligned_size(M,4) and numerical match vs native reference.

findings.claim: With tma_aligned_scales=True the scale tensor is over-allocated: it is sized to tma_aligned_m (M rounded up to multiple of 4) in the padded dimension via torch.empty_strided, so allocated storage is tma_aligned_m*sf_k floats rather than M*sf_k. This is a small extra allocation (at most 3 extra rows worth of float32 scales per call) but occurs on every activation quantization in the DeepGemm path. Impact is negligible for large M but non-zero.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocated scale-tensor bytes for small M (M=1,7) with tma_aligned_scales=True vs False.

findings.claim: This change removes the separate get_col_major_tma_aligned_tensor() post-processing call (a DeepGEMM transpose/layout kernel) by producing the TMA-aligned scale layout directly inside the quantization kernel. This should REDUCE latency by eliminating an extra device kernel/transpose per DeepGemm linear/quant call on Hopper. Net effect is a performance improvement, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/deepgemm/benchmark_fp8_block_dense_gemm.py comparing pre/post commit end-to-end quant+gemm latency on Hopper.

findings.claim: This one-line change flips the default MLA prefill backend from TRTLLM ragged deepseek prefill (use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill=True) to disabled (False). Per the commit message, the intent is to re-enable FA4 as the default MLA prefill backend. Whether this helps or hurts runtime performance is entirely hardware/kernel dependent: on SM100 (Blackwell) the docs previously listed TRT-LLM Ragged as 'Default on SM100' for DeepSeek R1 dims, so on those GPUs prefill will now route through the FA4 path instead of the TRTLLM ragged kernel. If FA4 prefill is slower than the TRTLLM ragged kernel for DeepSeek-shaped workloads on some SM100 configurations, MLA prefill throughput/latency could regress. The git history shows this default has been toggled back and forth several times (e.g. #38562 changed it back to TRT-LLM), indicating the relative performance is not stable across kernel/hardware combinations. This is a policy/default change; from the config diff alone the performance direction cannot be confirmed, only that it changes which prefill kernel is used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run benchmarks/attention_benchmarks/mla_runner.py for DeepSeek R1 dims on SM100 (Blackwell) comparing use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill=True vs False (FA4) across a range of prefill/context lengths; measure prefill latency and throughput to confirm FA4 default does not regress relative to the TRTLLM ragged kernel.

findings.claim: In `_dynamic_step_sample_logits_and_verify_tokens`, when `materialize_only_last_token_logits` is enabled, `required_logits = logits.squeeze(0)` is used directly (correct), but `context.speculative_required_logit_indices(logits.device)` is still called unconditionally to build the full index tensor. That helper runs `torch.cumsum` plus `torch.cat` and a `torch.arange` on the GPU every step even when the indices are no longer needed for slicing the logits. This is minor extra kernel-launch overhead per decode step, executed on the critical path of every speculative decode iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Speculative decode throughput (tokens/s) with materialize_only_last_token_logits=True vs the previous path, measuring per-step latency of index construction.

findings.claim: `speculative_required_logit_indices` recomputes `torch.cumsum(query_lengths)` and a `torch.arange` on every call. In the speculative path it is now called both from `last_token_logits` (during logits materialization) and again from `_dynamic_step_sample_logits_and_verify_tokens`, duplicating the same GPU cumsum/arange/cat work twice per step. Caching or passing the index tensor through would avoid the duplicate GPU kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count GPU kernel launches per decode step in speculative mode before/after; measure per-step latency.

findings.claim: The `@torch.inference_mode()` decorator on `async_generate_output_tokens_dynamic_batch` was replaced by two separate `with torch.inference_mode():` blocks, with the `await asyncio.sleep(0)` yield point placed between them (outside inference_mode). Entering/exiting inference_mode twice per step adds a small amount of per-step overhead (dispatcher mode push/pop) versus a single enclosing context. This is negligible but non-zero on a hot per-step path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark per-step Python overhead of double inference_mode enter/exit vs single, in a tight decode loop.

findings.claim: Enabling fuse_rope_kvcache adds three new Inductor graph passes (SplitCoalescingPass, ScatterSplitReplacementPass, RopeKVCacheFusionPass). RopeKVCacheFusionPass registers 2 replacement patterns (is_neox True/False) per attention layer, each requiring pattern tracing at pass construction. On models with many attention layers this measurably increases torch.compile warmup/startup time. This is compile-time only and gated behind the flag (ROCm-only), so runtime is unaffected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile cold-start time on a ROCm model with fuse_rope_kvcache=True vs False, scaling with number of attention layers.

findings.claim: When fuse_rope_kvcache is enabled and rope_kvcache_fusion_max_token_num (default 256) is below the max compile range end, a new compile-range split point is appended. This creates an additional compiled graph specialization (one range <=256 with fusion, one above without), roughly doubling compilation cost and CUDA graph capture memory for the affected range. This is the intended design (fusion only helps small-batch decode) but does increase startup cost and memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare number of compiled graphs and total CUDA graph capture memory with fuse_rope_kvcache on vs off.

findings.claim: get_attention_context (called once per attention layer per forward via unified_attention/unified_attention_with_output) now additionally reads forward_context.slot_mapping, runs an isinstance(...) assertion, and does a dict.get(layer_name). This adds a small constant Python overhead to every attention call for all backends, even those that never use the fused RoPE+KVCache path. Impact is minor per-call but occurs on the eager/direct-call hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark unified_attention dispatch overhead per layer with and without the extra slot_mapping fetch/assert.

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion now returns False whenever data_parallel_size > 1 (new `cfg.parallel_config.data_parallel_size == 1` gate). Previously the AllReduce+RMSNorm fusion was enabled for any TP>1 + Hopper + flashinfer configuration regardless of DP. As a result, TP>1 + DP>1 deployments on Hopper/Blackwell will no longer fuse the allreduce and RMSNorm/quant, adding back separate collective + normalization kernels on the critical per-layer path and reducing decode throughput. This is a deliberate correctness workaround (issue #34458), but it is nonetheless a measurable throughput regression for DP>1 setups that previously benefited from the fusion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput (tokens/s) of a dense TP=2,DP=2 model on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer installed, before vs after this commit, at -O2/-O3.

findings.claim: DeepseekV3ForCausalLM.verify_and_update_config now disables fuse_allreduce_rms by default when the model is NVFP4-quantized (is_nvfp4_quantized). This is inherited by DeepseekV32ForCausalLM as well. For DeepSeekV3/V3.2 NVFP4 checkpoints on TP>1 Hopper/Blackwell, the previously-enabled AllReduce+RMS fusion is now turned off, so those layers execute separate allreduce and RMSNorm/quant kernels, lowering throughput. Cited as a correctness workaround (issue #34395), but it is a real perf reduction for that model/quant combination.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSeek-V3 NVFP4 (modelopt_fp4) at TP>=2 on Blackwell before vs after; measure per-token latency and GPU util.

findings.claim: The device-capability check changed from has_device_capability(90) (which is a >= comparison, matching cap 9.0 and everything above, including 10.0/12.0 and future archs) to is_device_capability(100) or is_device_capability(90), which are exact-match checks. Architectures whose compute capability is not exactly 9.0 or 10.0 (e.g. SM 10.3 Blackwell variants, SM 12.0 consumer Blackwell, or future >10.0 GPUs) will now have the AllReduce+RMS fusion disabled where the old >=90 check would have enabled it, potentially leaving throughput on the table on those devices. Consider is_device_capability_family(100) / >=90 to preserve broad coverage.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM 10.3 or SM 12.0 device with flashinfer + TP>1, confirm whether fuse_allreduce_rms is still applied after the change.

findings.category: cpu-gpu-sync / behavior change on default path
findings.claim: get_grad_norm_fp32 now returns a GPU tensor (total_norm.pow(...)) instead of a Python float whenever multi_tensor_scale_tensor_impl is available (the Transformer Engine path). This branch is taken unconditionally, not just when --optimizer-cuda-graph is set. Downstream code in training.py logs grad_norm via reduce_max_stat_across_model_parallel_group and formats it with f'{grad_norm:.3f}', which will still force a CPU sync per step (or error / behave differently) but now the sync happens later and in more places, and any code that assumed a Python float (e.g. reduce_max_stat, tensorboard add_scalar) may trigger implicit host-device syncs at unexpected points. This changes the default (non-CUDA-graph) code path for all TE users, potentially adding/moving GPU->CPU synchronizations each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run standard training with TE installed (no --optimizer-cuda-graph) and compare per-step time and number of cudaStreamSynchronize calls before/after this commit.

findings.claim: dummy_overflow_buf = torch.zeros(1, dtype=torch.int, device='cuda') is now allocated on every call to clip_grad_by_total_norm_fp32, including the common case clip_coeff >= 1.0 where previously no allocation occurred. This is a small per-step CUDA allocation (and possible sync) that used to be skipped when no clipping was needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark clip_grad_by_total_norm_fp32 with clip_coeff>=1.0 measuring allocations/time per call before/after.

findings.claim: OptimizerCudaGraphWrapper captures optimizer.step() into a CUDA graph and replays it every subsequent iteration. The captured step includes gradient clipping / norm computation that involves collective all_reduce calls (get_grad_norm_fp32 does torch.distributed.all_reduce). NCCL collectives inside a captured graph are only safe if all ranks capture identically and replay in lock-step; if grad-norm/clip involves data-dependent control flow (e.g. clip_coeff<1.0 branch when clip_coeff is a Python float, or inf/nan skip logic), the replayed graph will use stale/incorrect control decisions, and mismatched capture across ranks can cause a hang. The wrapper unconditionally replays with no re-capture, so any per-iteration variation (loss scale changes, skipped steps on inf/nan) is frozen at capture time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable --optimizer-cuda-graph on a multi-GPU run with dynamic loss scaling and verify no hang and correct grad-norm/step-skip behavior; compare throughput vs eager optimizer.step.

findings.claim: OptimizerCudaGraphWrapper stores cuda_graph, result and curr_iteration as class-level (static) attributes rather than instance attributes. If more than one optimizer instance is wrapped (e.g. multiple optimizer chained/expert optimizers), they will share and clobber a single captured graph and result, leading to incorrect replays or holding onto a graph that pins CUDA memory for the wrong optimizer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run with chained optimizers (dense + expert) and --optimizer-cuda-graph; verify each optimizer's step is captured/replayed independently.

findings.claim: Two added self.stream.wait_stream(torch.cuda.current_stream()) calls in StaticBufferLoader force the copy stream to wait on the current stream before every static-buffer copy. Combined with the existing current_stream().wait_stream(self.stream) at the end, this serializes the input copy against the compute stream on every microbatch, reducing the copy/compute overlap the separate stream was intended to provide. Correctness fix, but adds per-microbatch synchronization cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark full-iteration CUDA graph data loading throughput (microbatches/s) with and without the added wait_stream calls.

findings.claim: The new block-overflow check in `_add_request` adds a small amount of Python-level work (arithmetic + `math.ceil`) on the request-admission hot path. This runs once per request add, which is negligible per call but is pure CPU overhead on the admission path. It accesses `self.context.kv_block_allocator.total_count` (a plain Python int), so no GPU sync is involved. Impact is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark request admission throughput (requests/sec) via repeated _add_request calls before/after the commit.

findings.claim: The new configuration-summary log block builds a fairly large list of formatted strings (including multiple get_mem_size_str/math.prod calls) unconditionally, even though it is only emitted when inference_config._verbose and rank==0. The string/list construction happens regardless of the verbose flag; only the final logging.info is guarded. This runs once at context construction (not on a hot path), so cost is one-time and small, but could be avoided by guarding the whole block on _verbose.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time DynamicInferenceContext construction with verbose=False before/after; expect negligible-to-small one-time difference.

findings.claim: Replacing torch.zeros_like with torch.empty_like removes zero-initialization of the MoE output buffer. This is a legitimate micro-optimization (skips a memset kernel launch / fill pass over the full (M,K) tensor) that saves GPU time and a kernel launch per MoE forward, PROVIDED the finalize/combine step writes every element of `output`. If any code path relies on the output being pre-zeroed — e.g. tokens routed to no local expert (expert-parallel sharding where a token's top-k experts are all remote), padding rows, or partial/scatter writes during combine — those positions will now contain uninitialized garbage instead of 0, which changes results and can propagate NaN/Inf. NaN/Inf propagation does not slow the kernel per se, but downstream code that guards against NaN (e.g. NaN checks, recomputation, or numerical-stability re-runs) could add overhead. The change is safe only if finalize fully overwrites the buffer; this cannot be confirmed from the diff alone since finalize is implementation-specific across many PrepareAndFinalize subclasses.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark a single FusedMoEModularKernel.forward on a large hidden_states tensor (e.g. M=8192, K=4096) comparing torch.zeros_like vs torch.empty_like allocation time and kernel-launch count; separately run an expert-parallel (EP>1) end-to-end correctness test to confirm all output rows are written by finalize and no uninitialized rows remain.

findings.claim: This commit reverts PR #33641 ("Significantly speed up cold start times"). The cache key reverts from a content-based `graph_hash` (SHA256 of the pickled subgraph) back to a positional `graph_index`. With `graph_hash`, identical subgraphs (e.g. the 32 structurally-identical transformer layers in a typical model) collapse to a single cache entry, so only the unique subgraphs are ever processed. The updated test confirms this: previously cold start produced 3 aot_autograd cache misses and 0 hits (the 30 duplicate layers were short-circuited by hash and never re-entered the compile pipeline); after the revert it produces 3 misses and 30 hits, i.e. all 30 duplicate subgraphs now traverse the full compile/load pipeline and only get short-circuited later by PyTorch's internal aot_autograd cache. This re-introduces per-duplicate-subgraph overhead during cold start (extra pipeline traversals, cache lookups, and graph loading), increasing model warm-up / first-request latency. The regression scales with the number of duplicated layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start compile time (VLLM_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE=1) for a multi-layer model such as microsoft/Phi-tiny-MoE-instruct before and after this commit; compare wall-clock LLM() construction time and the aot_autograd cache miss/hit counters.

findings.claim: The revert removes the `self.loaded_cache_entries` in-memory map and the early-return shortcut in `load()`. Previously, if a compiled graph for a given (range, hash, compiler) key had already been loaded/compiled in this process, `load()` returned it immediately without re-invoking `self.compiler.load()` (which does disk I/O / artifact deserialization). Without this map, repeated loads for equivalent graphs go through the compiler's `load()` path again, adding disk read and deserialization overhead during startup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time repeated CompilerManager.load() calls for identical subgraphs with a warm on-disk cache, before and after this commit.

findings.claim: The ROCm workaround adds two extra driver calls (cuMemAddressFree + cuMemAddressReserve) to every unmap_and_release invocation. During sleep() the Python layer calls unmap_and_release once per allocation, so the added VA-cycling doubles the address-space bookkeeping work per allocation. This is invoked serially under sleep/wake_up and only during sleep mode transitions (not on the hot inference path), so the aggregate cost is proportional to the number of allocations being freed; on a model with many chunks this makes sleep() measurably slower, but it is a one-time transition cost rather than a steady-state throughput regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock time of CuMemAllocator.sleep() on ROCm with a large model (many allocations/chunks) before and after this change to quantify the added driver-call latency.

findings.claim: cuMemAddressReserve is called with the alignment argument set to 0 (cuMemAddressReserve(&d_mem_new, size, 0, d_mem, 0)) whereas the original reservation in my_malloc used `granularity` as the alignment for ROCm. If the driver returns a different aligned address than the fixed hint d_mem, the code frees d_mem_new and bails with an error rather than retrying, so the entire VA region is now left with no reservation. On the subsequent wake_up, create_and_map maps into a VA that is no longer reserved, which can fail or fragment the address space; repeated sleep/wake cycles that hit this path could progressively lose usable VA / GPU memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run repeated sleep()/wake_up() cycles on ROCm and monitor GPU VRAM free-pool and VA reservation state (e.g. via rocm-smi) across many iterations to detect memory not being returned or growing reservation leaks.

findings.claim: If cuMemAddressFree succeeds but the following cuMemAddressReserve fails (first_error != no_error), the VA region has been freed but not re-reserved, and unmap_and_release returns via the CUDA_CHECK error path. On the next wake_up create_and_map will attempt to map into an unreserved VA, which will fail. This does not directly leak, but it can break the sleep/wake invariant and force fallback/reallocation, increasing memory pressure across cycles.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fault-inject a failing cuMemAddressReserve on ROCm and verify wake_up still succeeds without additional VRAM growth.

findings.claim: Selecting ckpt_fully_parallel_save_process_group='ep_dp' (or load='ep_dp') routes the fully-parallel save/load distribution through the expert-data-parallel group instead of the (larger) data-parallel+context-parallel group. The FullyParallelSave/LoadStrategyWrapper distributes shard I/O uniformly across the ranks of the given process group, so a smaller group means fewer ranks share the read/write and inter-rank exchange work, reducing checkpoint save/load throughput and increasing per-rank storage bandwidth demand versus the 'dp' default. Only impacts runs that opt into 'ep_dp'; the default ('dp') preserves prior behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure distributed checkpoint save/load wall-clock time for an MoE model with EP>1 comparing ckpt_fully_parallel_{save,load}_process_group='dp' vs 'ep_dp', across DP sizes.

findings.claim: The load path now threads exchange_algo=args.ckpt_fully_parallel_load_exchange_algo into FullyParallelLoadStrategyWrapper. The default stays 'broadcast', matching prior behavior, so no default regression. However 'gather_object' materializes whole loaded state dicts on every rank via all_gather_object, which serializes tensors to CPU and can substantially increase peak host memory and load latency at scale versus per-tensor broadcast. This is opt-in and warned against in arguments.py for numerical stability, but users choosing it for performance reasons may instead see memory/latency regressions on large checkpoints.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare fully-parallel load latency and peak host memory for exchange_algo in {broadcast, gather_rounds, gather_object} on a large checkpoint with DP>=8.

findings.claim: In flashinfer.py, `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu` (which returned the pre-populated `_seq_lens_cpu` field, typically built on CPU with `torch.from_numpy(seq_lens_np)` at no synchronization cost) was replaced with `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu()`. The new call unconditionally performs a GPU->CPU device-to-host copy, forcing an implicit CUDA synchronization on the attention-metadata build path every forward step. It also ignores the already-available CPU copy in `_seq_lens_cpu`. This defeats the very optimization the deprecated property's docstring describes ('may be expensive to retrieve in async mode') and stalls the CPU launch pipeline (breaks async scheduling / full-CUDA-graph decode overlap).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-heavy throughput/latency with FlashInfer backend and async scheduling enabled; measure per-step CPU->GPU stall via nsys and tokens/s vs parent.

findings.claim: In triton_attn.py the cascade-attention path now computes `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu() - common_prefix_len` instead of using the pre-populated `seq_lens_cpu`. The `.cpu()` call forces a device-to-host copy + implicit sync, then the result is immediately copied back to device with `.to(self.device)`. Previously the CPU tensor was already materialized without a sync. This adds an extra D->H synchronization to the cascade metadata build.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Triton attention backend with cascade attention enabled (shared long prefix); measure metadata build time and per-step latency vs parent.

findings.claim: In flashmla_sparse.py the prefill path now uses `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu()` instead of the pre-populated `seq_lens_cpu` property. This forces a fresh GPU->CPU copy and implicit sync per build for mixed/prefill batches, whereas the prior property reused the already-available `_seq_lens_cpu` CPU tensor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sparse MLA backend prefill/mixed batches; measure metadata build latency vs parent.

findings.claim: In mla/common.py the prefill branch now computes `common_attn_metadata.compute_num_computed_tokens().cpu()`. `compute_num_computed_tokens()` builds `seq_lens - query_lens` on the GPU (a device kernel launch) and then `.cpu()` copies it back to host, forcing an implicit synchronization. Previously `num_computed_tokens_cpu` was derived entirely on the CPU from the already-available `query_start_loc_cpu` and `_seq_lens_cpu` with no device sync. This adds both a GPU kernel and a D->H sync to the MLA prefill metadata build.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA backend (e.g. DeepSeek) prefill-heavy workload; measure metadata build latency and per-step stalls vs parent.

findings.claim: The new `compute_num_computed_tokens()` caches its result in `_num_computed_tokens_cache`, but this cache field is not copied/sliced in `unpadded()` or `_make_metadata_with_slice()`, so sliced metadata objects will recompute it on the GPU. Additionally, callers that immediately do `.cpu()` (mla/common.py) never benefit from the on-device cache while still paying the D->H sync each call.

flex_attention.py switched from `num_computed_tokens_cpu.to(device, non_blocking=True)` (an async CPU->GPU copy from an already-materialized CPU tensor) to `compute_num_computed_tokens()` which runs an elementwise subtraction kernel on GPU. This is likely neutral-to-positive (avoids a copy), but adds a device kernel launch to the build path; worth verifying it does not serialize with a prior sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FlexAttention backend build-path microbenchmark vs parent.

findings.claim: The eager state release that used to run at the end of every backward pass was removed. Previously `backward_impl` called `self._release_state()` synchronously, immediately nulling `before_detached`, `detached`, `layer_state`, `chunk_state`, and `submodule` (which pin activation tensors). Now that method is renamed to `__del__`, so those references are only dropped when the node object is garbage-collected (refcount reaches zero). Any lingering reference to a node — or the presence of `__del__` making the node part of a reference cycle collectable only by the generational GC rather than refcounting — delays freeing of the pinned activation memory, raising peak GPU memory. The commit tries to compensate by explicitly calling `release_state()`/`pop_layer()` on the schedule plan, but that relies entirely on there being no other references and on `__del__` firing promptly; if it doesn't, activations for the whole layer persist longer than before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a2a-overlap MoE training with the 1F1B overlap path and measure peak allocated GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) per iteration vs. parent commit; also gc.collect() timing / uncollectable object count.

findings.claim: When `ep_overlap_early_attn_memory_release` is enabled, `post_attn.backward` and `attn.backward` for the b_layer are moved to execute BEFORE `mlp.forward` of the f_layer instead of after `moe_combine.forward`. As the commit's own docstring states, this makes `moe_combine_fwd` and `moe_dispatch_bwd` become exposed (no longer overlapped with computation), trading throughput for lower peak memory. Enabling this flag will measurably reduce GPU utilization / increase step time on the EP A2A overlap path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-iteration wall-clock time and comm-overlap (nsys A2A exposure) with ep_overlap_early_attn_memory_release True vs False on an MoE EP-overlap config.

findings.claim: For HybridEP, `should_free_input` now returns `mlp: not enable_hybridep` (i.e. False) and `moe_dispatch: not (enable_deepep or enable_hybridep)` (False). This keeps the mlp input and dispatch input tensors alive through the backward pass for HybridEP, which is required for correctness but increases live activation memory relative to the plain alltoall dispatcher where those inputs were freed after forward. This is inherent to HybridEP but worth noting as a memory-footprint increase for that path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak memory of HybridEP dispatcher vs alltoall dispatcher on the same MoE model.

findings.category: assertion / potential correctness-safety on wgrad path
findings.claim: `backward_dw` now asserts `self.delay_grads_release`, which is only set True inside `backward_impl` when `delay_wgrad_compute` is enabled AND `len(self.bwd_dw_callables) > 0`. `manual_release_grads` is initialized to False and never set to True in this diff, so the `untyped_storage().resize_(0)` early-release branch is effectively dead code; the intended output-grad memory reduction from that path will not actually occur, meaning the advertised memory saving via manual dgrad release is not realized unless manual_release_grads is set elsewhere.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grep for where manual_release_grads is assigned True; if never set, verify peak memory is unchanged by the manual-release code path.

findings.claim: The change wraps torch.fx.passes.split_module.split_module in _use_lazy_graph_module(True) so that the created submodules (and the parent split GraphModule) defer their recompile() until first actually needed. The purpose is to avoid redundant Python code generation for the many submodules produced by piecewise splitting; recompile() would otherwise codegen forward() for every subgraph even when it is subsequently mutated/recompiled by later passes. This is a compile-time / startup-time optimization (fewer redundant recompiles during model warmup/torch.compile), and it does NOT affect steady-state runtime throughput or latency because compilation happens once during setup. Net effect on performance is neutral-to-positive; no runtime regression is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end torch.compile warmup / model-load time (e.g. time from engine init to first forward) with and without the lazy-graph-module wrapper on a piecewise-split model with many subgraphs; expect compile time to decrease or stay flat, and steady-state decode latency to be unchanged.

findings.claim: The optimization installs two context managers (torch._functorch.config.patch(autograd_cache_normalize_inputs=True) and unittest.mock.patch of autograd_cache_key) on every call to CompilerManager.compile(). Setting up/tearing down unittest.mock.patch and the functorch config patch per subgraph adds minor overhead to each backend compilation. This is compile-time only (not inference runtime) and the net effect is still a large compile-time win because it eliminates ~30 duplicate artifact compilations, so this is a very minor concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: tests/compile/test_cold_start.py cold-start wall-clock timing before/after the change

findings.claim: When a cache_key hits self.loaded_artifacts, the exact same compiled_graph object is returned for multiple distinct subgraphs (isometric graphs with autograd_cache_normalize_inputs=True). If the compiled callable holds any per-instance mutable state (e.g. lazily-initialized static input buffers as in make_copy_and_call, or cudagraph capture state), sharing one object across subgraphs could cause runtime correctness issues or unexpected re-capture/buffer contention. The wrapping in PiecewiseBackend appears to add its own per-range state, so this is likely safe, but reuse of a single stateful callable across graph positions is worth verifying to avoid latency regressions from repeated buffer re-init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end inference correctness + latency on a model with repeated identical layers (e.g. Phi-tiny-MoE) comparing per-token latency before/after

findings.claim: `Base._decorate_for_torch_compile` now calls `self._get_decoder_cls(**kwargs)` unconditionally during every Transformers-backend model init, and `_get_decoder_cls` does a full extra `AutoModel.from_config(**kwargs)` on the meta device just to call `model.get_decoder()` and read its type. This builds the entire model's module tree a second time at startup (in addition to the real `AutoModel.from_config` that follows). For large models with thousands of submodules, constructing the module graph twice measurably increases model-load/startup time, even though tensors are on meta device.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock time of TransformersForCausalLM.__init__ (model load) before vs. after this commit for a large HF model; compare with/without the extra AutoModel.from_config in _get_decoder_cls.

findings.claim: For multimodal models with `compile_mm_encoder=True`, `MultiModalMixin._decorate_for_torch_compile` performs yet another full `AutoModel.from_config(**kwargs)` on meta device in `_get_encoder_cls` to identify the encoder class, on top of the decoder-side extra instantiation and the real init instantiation. That is three full module-tree constructions at startup. This is gated behind the (default-off) `compile_mm_encoder` flag, so impact is limited to opted-in multimodal runs, but it further inflates load time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model-load time for a multimodal Transformers-backend model with compile_mm_encoder enabled, counting AutoModel.from_config invocations.

findings.claim: The compiled region moved from the vLLM wrapper `Base.forward` (which wrapped the whole `self.model(...)` HF call) to the HF *decoder* class's own forward via a runtime-generated `SupportTorchCompileWrapper` subclass patched into the HF module namespace. Wrapper-level work in `Base.forward` (batch-dim reshaping, embedding scaling, and for multimodal the vision-embedding merge) is now outside the compiled graph. This can change the graph boundaries and reduce fusion opportunities compared to the prior single compiled region; whether it degrades runtime depends on the model, so it warrants a throughput check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput/latency for a Transformers-backend model before vs. after this commit under CompilationMode with piecewise cudagraphs.

findings.claim: The refactor hoists the tex.thd_get_partitioned_indices() call out of the per-key loop and computes the index once using batch[next(iter(batch))].size(1). This is a legitimate optimization (avoids recomputing identical indices per tensor and removes redundant CUDA calls), but it only produces correct partitioning if every tensor in `batch` shares the same seq-length dimension (dim 1). If some entries in `batch` have a different size(1) than the first entry, index_select(1, index) will now use indices computed for the wrong length, which can cause out-of-bounds indexing errors or silently produce mismatched partitions -- not a slowdown per se, but the assumption of uniform seq length is now baked in. Assuming all batch tensors share the same padded seq length (which is the norm for THD packed data), the change is a net performance win: one thd_get_partitioned_indices call instead of N, reducing per-step CUDA launch/host overhead in the dataloader hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time _preprocess/context-parallel batch reshuffle for a LLaVA THD packed batch with many tensor keys and CP>1, comparing per-key vs single index computation.

findings.claim: In training.py the module-level `try: from torch_memory_saver import torch_memory_saver; torch_memory_saver.hook_mode = "torch"` sets the global torch_memory_saver hook mode unconditionally at import time whenever the package is installed, even when RL inference-model offloading is not being used. The 'torch' hook mode installs allocator hooks that intercept CUDA memory allocations, which can add per-allocation overhead to ALL CUDA allocations in the training process regardless of whether the offload feature is enabled. This is a side-effecting import that affects every training job that merely has the package installed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare training step throughput / allocator overhead with torch_memory_saver installed (hook_mode='torch' active) vs uninstalled, on a standard (non-RL-offload) training run.

findings.claim: The torch_memory_saver.region(tag=..., enable_cpu_backup=True) allocation used for the inference model reserves a full CPU-side backup buffer for the model weights. This permanently increases host (pinned/CPU) memory footprint by roughly the size of the inference model weights for the lifetime of the process, in addition to the GPU allocation. If the inference model is large this can be a substantial host memory cost that was not present in the UVM-only path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure host RSS with rl_offload_inference_model_weights_when_idle enabled via torch_memory_saver vs UVM path for a large inference model.

findings.claim: The torch_memory_saver pause/resume swap moves the entire inference-model weight set between CPU and GPU on every rollout/idle transition. Unlike the UVM path which uses managed prefetch (page migration), pause('rl_inference_model')/resume() performs a full copy of the region each cycle. If offload/restore happens frequently relative to rollout duration, the PCIe transfer of the full weight set each cycle adds latency to every inference boundary; there is no incremental/partial transfer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time per rollout cycle offload+restore latency with torch_memory_saver backend vs UVM backend for equal model sizes.

findings.claim: At optimization_level O0, the new KernelConfig default sets enable_flashinfer_autotune=False, which now skips FlashInfer autotuning during kernel warmup. Previously autotune ran unconditionally whenever FlashInfer was available and device capability >= 90 (Hopper/Blackwell), regardless of optimization level. Per flashinfer_autotune's own docstring, without autotuning FlashInfer falls back to heuristics that 'may be significantly slower.' So O0 runs on SM90/SM100 GPUs will use slower FlashInfer kernels at inference time than before this change. This is likely intentional (O0 = fastest startup, no optimization), but it is a runtime throughput/latency regression for that mode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a decode/prefill throughput benchmark on a Hopper or Blackwell GPU with FlashInfer backend at optimization_level=0 before vs after this commit; compare tokens/sec and per-step latency.

findings.claim: For O1/O2/O3 the enable_flashinfer_autotune default is True and the warmup path is unchanged, so no new steady-state runtime cost is introduced. The remaining diff is config plumbing (new KernelConfig dataclass, CLI arg, deepcopy of a tiny config in create_engine_config) with negligible one-time startup cost and no runtime-path effect.

In the CK branch of apply() (executed on every forward pass / every MoE layer invocation), rocm_aiter_ops.get_aiter_activation_type("swiglu") and get_aiter_quant_type("per_1x32") are called with constant string literals. Each call performs a `from aiter import ...` module attribute lookup, an isinstance() check, a .strip().lower() on the string, and constructs a fresh dict mapping every invocation before doing a dict.get(). These enum values are constant for the lifetime of the layer, so this is pure redundant work done in the latency-critical path. It should be computed once (e.g. in process_weights_after_loading or __init__) and cached on self. On a decode step with many MoE layers this adds measurable Python overhead per layer per token-batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench: call get_aiter_activation_type/get_aiter_quant_type in a tight loop vs. a cached attribute lookup; also profile MoE forward latency with the CK backend comparing cached vs per-call enum resolution.

findings.claim: The pad values passed to fused_moe are recomputed every forward call: `self.hidden_pad // 128 * 128` and `self.intermediate_pad // 64 * 64 * 2`. These depend only on load-time constants and could be precomputed once in process_weights_after_loading / create_weights. Minor CPU overhead but trivially avoidable in the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Negligible on its own; fold into MoE forward latency measurement.

findings.claim: rocm_aiter_fused_topk is registered as a custom op whose declared return type is tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor], but its fake impl (_rocm_aiter_fused_topk_fake) just `pass`es and returns None instead of returning correctly-shaped meta tensors. Under torch.compile / fake-tensor tracing this can cause a graph break or fall back to eager for the topk step in the MoE path, degrading captured-graph performance for the CK backend. Other fake impls that return tensors (e.g. _rocm_aiter_fused_moe_fake) return torch.empty_like; this one should return appropriately-shaped empty tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace the CK MoE apply() path under torch.compile and check for graph breaks around rocm_aiter_fused_topk; compare captured-graph vs eager latency.

findings.claim: `hash_to_rank_info` is an unbounded dict that accumulates every block hash ever seen for the entire lifetime of the coordinator process, mapping each to a dict of {rank_identity -> timestamp}. There is no eviction, TTL, or size cap. Under sustained inference with prefix caching enabled, the number of distinct prefix-block hashes grows without bound (each new unique block prefix adds an entry), so the coordinator's resident memory grows monotonically with the total number of unique prompt blocks processed. Since the coordinator is a long-lived single process, this is a slow memory leak that can OOM the coordinator over long-running serving.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the coordinator with enable_prefix_caching and stream a large number (millions) of requests with distinct prompt prefixes; monitor RSS of the coordinator process over time.

findings.claim: In the default FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK policy, only the first block hash is needed (`request_hashes = request_hashes[:1]`), but `compute_request_hashes` first computes hashes for ALL complete blocks of the whole prompt (tokenize + torch.tensor allocation + full matmul + tolist + accumulate over all blocks) before the slice discards everything after index 0. For long prompts this performs O(seq_len) work per request where O(block_size) would suffice, and it runs inline in the coordinator's single-threaded ZMQ event loop, adding per-request latency and reducing coordinator throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure coordinator request-forwarding throughput (requests/sec) with FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK policy for short vs. very long prompts, comparing against round_robin.

findings.claim: Prefix-cache hash computation is now performed synchronously inside the coordinator's SUBMIT_REQUEST handler for every incoming request (tokenizing strings, allocating a torch.tensor, running compute_block_hashes_batched). The coordinator is a single-threaded event loop that fans out to all DP ranks; adding per-request CPU work here serializes behind every request and can become a throughput bottleneck at high request rates, whereas previously routing was O(1) round-robin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak requests/sec through the coordinator with enable_prefix_caching=True vs False under a saturating client load.

findings.claim: When `coordinator_schedule_output_path` is set, `schedule_records` appends one dict per request for the entire run and is only flushed to disk at stop(). For long serving runs this list grows unbounded in memory and the final json.dump serializes the whole list at once, causing a memory spike and potential latency stall at shutdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable schedule_output_path and run a long request stream; monitor coordinator RSS growth and time spent in stop()/json.dump.

findings.claim: `hash_to_rank_info` records every past assignment as ground truth of what a rank holds, but it never reflects actual KV-cache evictions on the engine. Over time the shadow state diverges from real cache contents, so LONGEST_PREFIX/FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK routing can send requests to ranks that have already evicted the matching prefix, yielding cache misses and degrading the effective prefix-cache hit rate (and thus prefill throughput) that the feature was intended to improve.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end prefill cache-hit rate / TTFT under a workload with prefix reuse and cache pressure (evictions), comparing coordinator-routed vs. actual engine cache state.

findings.claim: offload_grad_buffers() defaults to empty_cache=True and synchronize=True and is now invoked every rollout-collection cycle (once in get_environment_rollouts and again inside megatron_rl_inference_mode at the other call site). torch.cuda.empty_cache() returns the freed grad-buffer memory to the CUDA driver, so restore_grad_buffers() must re-acquire it via cudaMalloc, and any other allocations that grow into that space will cause the grad buffers to land in a different (possibly fragmented) region on reload. This adds a full-device synchronize + cudaFree/cudaMalloc churn on the critical path of every inference/training transition, which is more expensive than simply resize_(0)/resize_(size) without empty_cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock of the offload/restore transition per rollout iteration with empty_cache=True vs False, and track cudaMalloc/cudaFree counts and allocator fragmentation over many RL iterations.

findings.claim: get_environment_rollouts now calls model[0].offload_grad_buffers() (which does torch.cuda.synchronize() + empty_cache()) followed by optimizer.offload_to_cpu() (which itself calls empty_cache per optimizer.py:362). This yields two full-device synchronizations and two empty_cache passes per transition instead of the single empty_cache the previous code performed. The extra torch.cuda.synchronize() stalls the host until all pending GPU work drains, serializing what could otherwise overlap and adding latency to every rollout boundary.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the offload phase with NVTX ranges; compare number of synchronize/empty_cache calls and total transition latency before vs after the change.

findings.claim: reload_from_cpu() reallocates grad_data storage and then calls grad_data.zero_() over the entire buffer every restore. Zeroing the full grad buffer is an extra memset over the whole gradient memory each rollout transition; for large models this is a non-trivial GPU kernel that runs on the critical path. If the grads are always re-zeroed at the start of the next backward anyway, this is redundant work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark reload_from_cpu() time as a function of grad buffer size, and check whether the subsequent training step already zeroes grads.

findings.claim: On the move_params path, the first offload does self.param_data.cpu().pin_memory(), which allocates a pageable host buffer via .cpu() and then a second pinned host buffer via .pin_memory() (double allocation + double copy). Allocating pinned memory is itself expensive (page-locking). Although the DDP grad-buffer wrappers pass move_params=False, other callers using move_params=True pay this cost on the first offload.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time offload_to_cpu(move_params=True) first-call vs steady-state; compare against a direct pinned allocation + copy_ path.

findings.claim: The old FastIncrementalDetokenizer only primed the DecodeStream with at most the last ~24 prompt tokens (searching a suffix window of tokens 4..24 for a safe non-� start). The new code passes the entire request.prompt_token_ids to DecodeStream(ids=...), so for long prompts the native prefill now processes every prompt token instead of a bounded 24-token suffix. Although the native (Rust) prefill is far faster per token than the previous Python step loop, the total work is now proportional to full prompt length rather than capped, so for very long prompts the per-request construction cost could grow relative to the old capped behavior. In practice this is very likely still faster overall (native vs. Python loop + up to ~20 tokenizer.decode() calls), so this is low-confidence.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FastIncrementalDetokenizer construction time (from_new_request) with very long prompts (e.g. 8k-32k tokens) comparing old suffix-priming vs new native full-prompt prefill.

findings.claim: The USE_FAST_DETOKENIZER version gate was raised from tokenizers>=0.21.1 to >=0.22.0. Environments running tokenizers 0.21.1<=v<0.22.0 that previously used the fast Rust-based DecodeStream detokenizer will now fall back to the pure-Python SlowIncrementalDetokenizer, which is measurably slower per generated token. This is a necessary correctness constraint for the new ids= prefill API, but it does degrade detokenization throughput for those users until they upgrade tokenizers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-token detokenization latency of FastIncrementalDetokenizer vs SlowIncrementalDetokenizer to quantify the regression for users pinned to tokenizers 0.21.x.

findings.claim: The change adds `not accumulate_into_main_grad` to the condition that produces `[None]*num_groups` placeholder gradients. When accumulating into main_grad the code now returns the real `grad_weights` list rather than None placeholders. This is a correctness/memory-bookkeeping change: when accumulating into main_grad the wgrad is written directly into the pre-allocated main_grad buffer, so autograd no longer needs to allocate and accumulate a separate `.grad` tensor per group. If the previous code passed None here it forced autograd's default accumulation path, causing extra per-group gradient buffer allocation and an accumulate-add on every backward. The new code avoids that redundant allocation + add. This is a performance improvement, not a regression.

In non-cudagraph (eager) mode, the previous code did `paged_kv_indices = block_table_tensor[mask]`, producing a tightly-sized tensor of exactly the number of used pages (num_actual_pages). The new code instead always sets `paged_kv_indices = self.paged_kv_indices`, which is the full persistent buffer of size `max_num_reqs * max_model_len` (max_num_pages). This full-length buffer is then handed to the downstream MLA decode kernel via `attn_metadata.decode.paged_kv_indices`. In the eager path there is no slicing back to `num_actual_pages`, so the kernel receives a much larger indices tensor than needed. Depending on how `rocm_aiter_ops.mla_decode_fwd` uses `paged_kv_indices`, this can either (a) cause the kernel to process/iterate over the entire max-length buffer (large slowdown proportional to max_model_len rather than actual seq len), or (b) at minimum keep a large persistent buffer resident and touched every step. Combined with the added per-step `self.paged_kv_indices.fill_(-1)` (only in the cudagraph path) and the always-run Triton kernel, this changes the effective decode work from O(actual pages) toward O(max pages).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run AITER MLA decode on ROCm in eager (non-cudagraph) mode with short sequences but a large --max-model-len; compare decode step latency and paged_kv_indices tensor size before/after this commit.

findings.claim: The Triton `_copy_page_indices_kernel` uses `cu_num_blocks` (paged_kv_indptr) to compute per-request start/end offsets into `page_indices`. `paged_kv_indptr` is derived from `seq_lens_device.cumsum()`. But the kernel reads block ids from `block_table + req_idx * block_table_stride` for `num_blocks = end-start` entries, i.e. it assumes each request's number of valid pages equals its seq_len. The old boolean-mask code masked with `arange < seq_lens`, which is the same assumption, so functionally equivalent. However, note the kernel loop uses `BLOCK_SIZE=1024` and iterates `range(0, num_blocks, 1024)`; for typical decode where num_blocks==seq_len can be large (e.g. thousands), this loop plus the fill_(-1) over the full max_num_pages buffer runs every decode step, replacing a single fused gather. If the aiter kernel does not actually require the trailing -1 padding, the extra full-buffer fill is pure overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _build_decode() alone with realistic batch sizes and long contexts; measure wall time and CUDA kernel count per call before/after.

findings.claim: The persistent `self.paged_kv_indices` buffer of size `max_num_reqs * max_model_len * 4 bytes` is now always allocated, even when full cudagraphs are not used (previously it was only allocated inside the `has_full_cudagraphs()` branch). For large max_model_len and max_num_seqs this is a non-trivial, permanently resident GPU allocation added to the eager path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare steady-state GPU memory usage of the AITER MLA builder in eager mode with large max_model_len before/after this commit.

findings.claim: In the common case where num_outputs <= chunk_size (default 128), the old code passed the original list `outputs.outputs` directly (wrapped in a tuple) with zero copying. The new code always does `engine_core_outputs[start:end]`, which for a single chunk still allocates a new shallow list copy of up to `chunk_size` element references on every engine step. This is a small per-step allocation/copy that did not exist before. The cost is negligible (O(num_outputs) pointer copies) compared to the downstream process_outputs work, and is offset by removing numpy's object-array conversion in the multi-chunk path, so overall the change is net-neutral to positive. Only flagged for completeness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the output_handler loop with num_outputs values below and above 128 (e.g., 64, 128, 512) measuring per-step wall time / allocations, comparing list-slicing vs the previous np.array_split approach.

findings.claim: Forcing prefetch_bucket_size to 1 (engine.py, in _configure_zero_optimizer when zero_allgather_sequential is True) cripples the prefetch pipeline. In PartitionedParameterCoordinator.fetch_sub_module, max_params_to_prefetch = min(max_n_available - n_available, prefetch_bucket_sz). With prefetch_bucket_sz=1, numel_prefetching < max_params_to_prefetch effectively lets at most 1 element of prefetch through, so upcoming parameters are essentially never prefetched. This serializes communication and computation, eliminating the compute/allgather overlap that hides communication latency, and will significantly reduce GPU utilization and throughput for ZeRO-3 training.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ZeRO-3 training step time / tokens-per-sec with stage3_allgather_sequential=true vs false on a multi-billion-param model, measuring allgather/compute overlap and GPU util.

findings.claim: The sequential allgather path (_all_gather_sequential in partition_parameters.py, selected when self.allgather_sequential is True) issues one collective (allgather) per parameter instead of coalescing many parameters into a single bucketed collective. For models with many small parameters this multiplies the number of NCCL collective launches by the parameter count per submodule, inflating per-collective launch/latency overhead and kernel-launch overhead. Throughput will degrade in the common case (memory not the bottleneck); the optimization only helps peak memory, at a real latency cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare wall-clock forward+backward time and number of NCCL allgather calls for a transformer block with sequential vs coalesced allgather across 8 GPUs.

findings.claim: In _allgather_params_sequential, a fresh flat_tensor of size partition_size*num_partitions is allocated with torch.empty inside the per-param loop for every parameter, rather than a single shared buffer. This increases the number of allocator calls and can raise transient allocation/free churn compared to the previous single-buffer coalesced allocation, potentially increasing fragmentation and allocator overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocator activity (torch.cuda memory stats: num_alloc_retries, reserved bytes) during model init/gather with sequential path enabled.

findings.claim: The log_dist message 'If zero_allgather_sequential is True, set prefetch_bucket_size to 1' is emitted during optimizer configuration and mutates the config silently overriding a user-set stage3_prefetch_bucket_size; while cheap, forcibly overriding the prefetch bucket without regard to the user's value removes the user's ability to retain any prefetch overlap, which is the actual performance mechanism being disabled.

A new innermost loop `for is_tma_aligned in [False, True]` was added around the group-quant fusion pattern registration, doubling the number of registered FusedAddRMSNormGroupQuantPattern / RMSNormGroupQuantPattern patterns (now iterating group_shape x has_col_major_scales x is_e8m0 x is_tma_aligned x 2 epsilons => 2x more patterns than before). More registered patterns means the Inductor PatternMatcherPass has more subgraph patterns to attempt to match/replace during torch.compile, increasing compilation/warmup time. This is a one-time startup cost, not a steady-state runtime regression, and many of the added patterns (is_tma_aligned combined with non-col-major scales) may never match. Consider skipping registration of combinations that cannot occur (e.g. is_tma_aligned requires has_col_major_scales).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure RMSNormQuantFusionPass construction time and total torch.compile warmup time before/after (count of registered patterns).

findings.claim: In the transposed-scale store path of norm_and_quant and compute_dynamic_per_token_scales, the scale index now computes `scale_rows = (gridDim.x + outer_scale_stride - 1) / outer_scale_stride * outer_scale_stride` (an int64 division + multiply) instead of just using gridDim.x. `scale_rows` is loop-invariant so nvcc should hoist it out of the per-element loop, but in the group_size>0 vectorized store path it is recomputed within the store code region for each qualifying element. When outer_scale_stride==1 (the common non-TMA case) this yields the identical value as before at a small extra arithmetic cost. Impact is expected to be negligible after compiler hoisting, but worth confirming the division is not left inside the hot loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rms_norm_per_block_quant with transposed scales, outer_scale_stride=1, comparing kernel time before/after; inspect PTX/SASS for hoisting of the division.

findings.claim: For tma_alignment != 0, scales are allocated via torch.empty_strided with tma_aligned_m rounded up to the alignment (padded rows), so the scale buffer is slightly larger than the unpadded (M, sf_k) buffer. This is a small extra memory footprint required for TMA-aligned GEMM consumption, not a hot-path cost, but it does allocate padding elements that are never written by the kernel (uninitialized padding). Confirm downstream GEMM does not read those padding entries in a way that requires zeroing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak GPU memory of scale tensors with tma_alignment=4 vs 0 for typical M, and verify GEMM correctness/perf with padded scale layout.

findings.claim: The backend-eligibility conditions in nvte_get_fused_attn_backend are broadened so that more mask/window configurations (e.g. NVTE_NO_MASK with window_size_right==0, PADDING_MASK with arbitrary right windows, and general (left, right) SWA) are now routed to the cuDNN FusedAttention arbitrary-seqlen kernel instead of falling back to UnfusedDotProductAttention. This is intended to be faster, but for cases where the previously-selected path was actually competitive, routing to a newly-enabled cuDNN SWA config could select a suboptimal cuDNN kernel/plan. The change is functional-feature-driven; net effect on throughput depends on cuDNN's plan quality for the new (left,right) diagonal-band configs and should be benchmarked.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused attention fwd+bwd throughput for SWA (left,right) windows and for NO_MASK/PADDING_MASK configs newly routed to cuDNN, comparing against the pre-change backend selection on sm90/sm100.

findings.claim: set_diagonal_alignment and set_diagonal_band_right_bound are now unconditionally set on the cuDNN sdpa options (forward and backward). Setting a finite right band (window_size_right != -1) instructs cuDNN to build a two-sided diagonal-band mask, which can select a different (potentially slower or higher-workspace) plan than the previous one-sided causal/left-band-only path. For genuine two-sided SWA this is required, but the added set_diagonal_alignment(TOP_LEFT) on the common path should be verified to not change plan selection for the pre-existing causal/left-window cases.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare cuDNN plan selection and per-kernel latency/workspace for causal and (left,0) windows before vs after adding set_diagonal_alignment and set_diagonal_band_right_bound.

findings.claim: When --create-all-gather-group is enabled, an additional NCCL communicator (_DATA_PARALLEL_GROUP_WITH_CP_AG) is created over the identical dp-cp rank set. The model_weight_buffer all-gather is switched to this new group while the reduce-scatter stays on the original group. The intent is overlap, but running two independent NCCL communicators concurrently over the same physical links contends for the same NIC/NVLink bandwidth and SM resources; without careful stream/priority/SM-partition tuning this can reduce aggregate throughput and cause head-of-line/deadlock-adjacent stalls rather than improve it. There is no accompanying high_priority_stream / max_ctas partitioning wired for this new group (it reuses the plain 'dp_cp' nccl_options), so the two collectives will compete for the same SM budget.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP training step time (forward all-gather + backward reduce-scatter overlap) with and without --create-all-gather-group at fixed dp_cp size, measuring NCCL bus bandwidth and iteration latency.

findings.claim: Creating a second NCCL communicator over the full dp-cp rank set (group_with_cp_ag) permanently allocates extra NCCL communicator state (buffers, channels, NVLS/registration resources) for every rank, roughly doubling the per-communicator NCCL memory footprint of the dp-cp group even when the overlap benefit is marginal. This is a fixed memory cost paid when the flag is on.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GPU memory reserved for NCCL communicators (nvidia-smi / torch cuda memory summary) at init time with create_all_gather_group True vs False.

findings.claim: The new AG group is appended to self.ubr_groups so the shared NCCL mem pool gets registered with an additional communicator via MultiGroupUBRAllocator. Each extra group in ubr_groups triggers an additional deregister/register_mem_pool pass during buffer allocation, adding registration overhead at startup proportional to the number of groups; with the AG group this is an extra full mem-pool (de)registration per bucket allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure ParamAndGradBuffer construction / user-buffer-registration time with and without the AG group appended to ubr_groups.

findings.claim: utils.py now imports megatron.core.parallel_state at module import time inside the standalone Megatron-FSDP utils module. This introduces a hard-to-avoid import of the whole parallel_state module (and its transitive numpy/einops/torch imports) whenever utils is imported in the Megatron-Core context; negligible at runtime but adds a one-time import cost and couples the standalone module to Megatron-Core.

The new default `fsdp_all_gather_in_start_param_sync=True` preserves the prior behavior (prefetching the first parameter bucket before the forward pass), so the default is not a regression. However, when a user sets it to False, the else-branch that used to run in the `not overlap_param_gather` path (`synchronize_param_gather()` + iterating all buckets) is NOT executed — the code silently skips all initial parameter gathering. In the overlapped path the only initial gather was of the first bucket; with the flag False no initial all-gather is issued at all. If the FSDP prefetch pipeline relied on that first-bucket gather being kicked off before forward, disabling it removes the compute/communication overlap of the first bucket, so the initial all-gather cost is no longer hidden and the forward pass will stall waiting for the first bucket's parameters. This makes the flag-False path slower per the docstring's own claim about hiding initial communication cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Train a small FSDP (optim_grads_params) model for a few steps with fsdp_all_gather_in_start_param_sync=True vs False, measuring step-1/first-forward latency and end-to-end throughput to quantify lost overlap of the first bucket all-gather.

findings.claim: In the new naive_block_assignment path, _get_token_offs returns tl.where(offs == 0, pid_m, num_valid_tokens), so each launched M-block contains exactly one valid token row (row 0) while the remaining BLOCK_SIZE_M-1 rows are masked out. The kernel still computes a full BLOCK_SIZE_M x BLOCK_SIZE_N tile via tl.dot, so ~(BLOCK_SIZE_M-1)/BLOCK_SIZE_M of the FLOPs and A-loads per block are wasted. For BLOCK_SIZE_M=16..64 this is a 15x-63x arithmetic-efficiency loss per block relative to a dense-packed tile. This is bounded by the SPARSITY_FACTOR guard (only used when tokens*top_k is tiny vs experts*loras), so it is a deliberate tradeoff vs the padded/sorted path, but it can regress throughput if BLOCK_SIZE_M is large or the heuristic mis-fires.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_lora.py comparing naive vs sorted path across num_tokens/top_k/num_experts/max_loras sweeps, measuring latency and achieved TFLOPs for the shrink/expand kernels near the SPARSITY_FACTOR boundary.

findings.claim: In the naive path the num_tokens_post_padded early-exit check is skipped (guarded by `if not naive_block_assignment`). Combined with grid axis-0 = cdiv(EM, BLOCK_SIZE_M) where EM = num_tokens * BLOCK_SIZE_M, this launches exactly num_tokens M-blocks with no possibility of early termination for empty/padded blocks. If the SPARSITY_FACTOR heuristic underestimates activation density, blocks that would have been skipped in the sorted path now all execute a full-tile GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure total launched-block count and kernel time for naive vs sorted path as num_tokens approaches the SPARSITY_FACTOR threshold.

findings.claim: The refactor changes the per-block row offset from `tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE_M).to(tl.int32)` to `offs = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE_M).to(tl.int64)`, which is now used for address arithmetic in both the naive and the pre-existing sorted path. Promoting token/address index math to int64 increases register pressure and address-computation cost in the inner loop of the existing (previously int32) code path, a small but universal overhead applied even when the naive path is not taken.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare the sorted-path shrink/expand kernel latency before/after this commit with identical inputs to isolate the int32->int64 offset change.

findings.claim: In the new OFFLOAD path of `deallocate_inference_state_buffers`, the code does `self._offloadable_cpu_backups[name].copy_(tensor, non_blocking=True)` immediately followed by `tensor.storage().resize_(0)`. Because the D2H copy is issued as non-blocking on the current stream and there is no `torch.cuda.synchronize()` (or stream sync) between the copy and the storage resize, the GPU storage may be freed/resized to 0 before the async copy has actually read it. This can corrupt the CPU backup or, in the previous behavior with torch_memory_saver, this offload was managed by the library; the manual path introduces a correctness/perf hazard that may require an extra full synchronize to be safe, adding latency to every suspend.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Repeated suspend/resume cycles with kv_cache_management_mode=offload, measuring D2H/H2D copy correctness and suspend latency; compare against torch_memory_saver path.

findings.claim: For OFFLOAD mode without torch_memory_saver, `_allocate_memory_buffer` and `_allocate_mamba_states` now eagerly allocate a full pinned CPU backup (`torch.empty_like(...).pin_memory()`) equal in size to the KV cache and Mamba states at initialization time, and keep it resident for the process lifetime. Pinned host memory of this magnitude (potentially many GB) is a scarce resource; it is allocated even though it is only used during suspend windows, increasing host memory pressure versus the prior torch_memory_saver-only approach that managed backups internally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident pinned host memory after context init with kv_cache_management_mode=offload and no torch_memory_saver, comparing to persist mode.

findings.claim: The resume path now recaptures CUDA graphs whenever `kv_cache_management_mode != PERSIST` and `static_kv_memory_pointers` is False, matching the old `unified_memory_level==0 and not persist_cuda_graphs` condition. This is logically preserved, but note that in OFFLOAD mode (which keeps static addresses via resize_ rather than realloc) the KV pointers are actually stable, yet graphs are still deleted and recaptured on every suspend/resume unless the user also sets static_kv_memory_pointers=True. If users migrate offload_kv_cache=True (old) to OFFLOAD without setting static_kv_memory_pointers, they will incur full CUDA graph recapture cost on every resume, which is expensive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resume() capture_time in OFFLOAD mode with and without static_kv_memory_pointers=True across suspend/resume cycles.

findings.claim: In the new fused forward, `mixed_qkv, z = mixed_qkvz.split([qkv_size, z_size], dim=-1)` produces column slices of a 2D tensor that are non-contiguous (non-unit row stride). `mixed_qkv` is subsequently passed to gdn_attention_core (where rearrange_mixed_qkv splits/rearranges it) and `z` is reshaped; both operations may force an implicit copy of the sliced views. In the pre-change code these tensors came directly from separate contiguous GEMM outputs. This adds a possible extra tensor copy per layer per forward. This is minor relative to the offsetting benefit of fusing 4 GEMMs into 2, so the net effect is still a speedup, but the split-view contiguity is worth confirming.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark Qwen3.5 GDN layer forward (decode + prefill) comparing pre/post commit: measure per-layer latency and count of contiguous() copies; verify the 4->2 GEMM fusion nets a throughput win and the split does not introduce a large hidden copy.
